It formally expresses that the next state of the game depends on both the *previous state* and the *player's current input*. Without the $I_t$ term, the system is non-interactive (like a movie playing on autopilot).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Game State Vector** | $S_t$ | Multi-dimensional state at time $t$ |
| **State Transition** | $S_{t+1} = R(S_t, I_t)$ | Where $R$ is the rule function, $I_t$ is input |
| **Frame Rate (FPS)** | $\text{FPS} = \dfrac{1}{\Delta t}$ | Frames per second; inversely related to $\Delta t$ |
| **Response Latency** | $L = t_{feedback} - t_{input}$ | Measured in **milliseconds (ms)** |
| **Interactivity (Crawford)** | $I = f(\text{Speed, Range, Mapping})$ | Three-axis composite metric |
| **Frame Time** | $\Delta t = \dfrac{1}{\text{FPS}}$ | Seconds per frame; e.g., 16.67 ms at **60 FPS** |
| **Input Polling Rate** | $P_r$ | Hz (Hertz); e.g., a mouse polls at **1000 Hz** |
| **State Space Size** | $\vert S \vert = n \times m$ | Where $n$ = number of entities, $m$ = attributes per entity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember:** Crawford's three axes are **Speed, Range, and Mapping**. Speed is measured in **milliseconds (ms)**, and a standard smooth gameplay frame rate is **60 FPS** (16.67 ms per frame).

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Interactivity Theory

Interactivity principles are used extensively in:

- **Game Engines** (Unity, Unreal Engine) — the GameObject Update() loop and the Input System are practical implementations of $S_{t+1} = R(S_t, I_t)$.
- **Virtual Reality (VR) / Augmented Reality (AR)** — sub-millisecond latency is required to prevent motion sickness; this is the "Speed" axis pushed to its physical limit.
- **Human-Computer Interaction (HCI)** — interface designers use Crawford's Range and Mapping axes to design dashboards and productivity tools.
- **Simulation Training** — flight simulators, surgical trainers, and military simulations are built entirely on the interaction loop architecture.

## 2.5 Degrees and Types of Interactivity

Not all interactivity is equal. KTU syllabus distinguishes:

| Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Player ↔ Game** | Player directly affects the game world | Moving a character with WASD keys |
| **Player ↔ Player** | Players interact with each other through the game | Multiplayer combat, co-op puzzles |
| **Game ↔ Game** | Automated systems interact with each other | AI factions battling, economy simulation |
| **Player ↔ System UI** | Player interacts with menus and HUD | Pausing, opening inventory, adjusting settings |

> [!NOTE]
> The strongest game designs engage **all four** types simultaneously. For example, *Civilization VI* features player-game (city building), player-player (diplomacy), game-game (AI civilizations warring), and player-UI interactions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Interaction Loop as a Recurrence Relation

The interactive state transition $S_{t+1} = R(S_t, I_t)$ can be expanded step-by-step to reveal its **recursive structure**.

### Step 1 — Define the initial state

At game start (time $t = 0$), the system has an initial state vector $S_0$ supplied by the developer's level design. The player has not yet acted, so $I_0 = \emptyset$ (empty input).

$$
S_0 = \begin{bmatrix} x_0 \\ y_0 \\ z_0 \\ \text{score}_0 \\ \text{health}_0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 100 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the rule function for the first player input

The player presses a key. The input vector is $I_1$. The rule function $R$ is a composite of: physics, collision, AI, and game logic. Applying the transition:

$$
S_1 = R(S_0, I_1)
$$

Expanding the rule function for a simple movement example (player presses "W" to move forward by $\Delta x$):

$$
S_1 = R\!\left( \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 100 \end{bmatrix}, \begin{bmatrix} \Delta x \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \right) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 + \Delta x \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 100 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This row explains the logic: the input vector is added to the position components of the state, while the other components (score, health) remain unchanged because no scoring or damage event occurred.

### Step 3 — Unfold the recurrence for N frames

Applying the rule function repeatedly, we obtain the **unfolded recurrence**:

$$
S_N = R\bigl(R(R(\cdots R(S_0, I_0) \cdots , I_{N-2}), I_{N-1}), I_N\bigr)
$$

This can be written compactly using the **iterated function notation**:

$$
S_N = R^N(S_0, I_0, I_1, I_2, \ldots, I_N)
$$

Where $R^N$ denotes the $N$-fold application of the rule function. This row shows that the final state of the game depends on the *entire history* of player inputs — a mathematical proof that player agency is woven into every pixel of the result.

### Step 4 — Derive the frame time constraint

A game running at **60 FPS** must complete one full interaction loop (input → process → render) within:

$$
\Delta t_{\max} = \frac{1 \text{ second}}{60 \text{ frames}} = 0.01667 \text{ s} = 16.67 \text{ ms}
$$

If the processing stage $R(S_t, I_t)$ takes longer than $\Delta t_{\max}$, the game *misses frames* and the player perceives **stutter or lag**, which directly damages the **Speed axis** of interactivity.

### Step 5 — Derive the interaction latency budget

A well-designed game allocates the **16.67 ms budget** as follows:

$$
t_{input} + t_{process} + t_{render} \leq 16.67 \text{ ms}
$$

Typical KTU-style allocation:

$$
t_{input} \approx 1 \text{ ms}, \quad t_{process} \approx 8 \text{ ms}, \quad t_{render} \approx 7 \text{ ms}, \quad \text{buffer} \approx 0.67 \text{ ms}
$$

This row shows the standard *triple-buffered* breakdown. If any component exceeds its budget, the game drops below **60 FPS** and interactivity quality degrades.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Interactive Media

| Property | Book | Film | Game (Interactive) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Player input required?** | No | No | **Yes** |
| **State changes with input?** | No | No | **Yes** |
| **State transition formula** | $S_{t+1} = S_t$ | $S_{t+1} = S_t$ | $S_{t+1} = R(S_t, I_t)$ |
| **Bidirectional flow?** | No | No | **Yes** |
| **Non-linear narrative possible?** | Reader-paced only | No | **Yes** |
| **Feedback type** | Imagined | Sensory (visual/audio) | Sensory + responsive |

> [!NOTE]
> The defining mathematical signature of interactivity is the presence of the input term $I_t$ in the state transition equation. A medium where $I_t$ is absent is *not* interactive.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Core Interaction Loop (Mermaid Flowchart)

This diagram visualizes the **Input → Process → State Update → Output** loop that defines interactivity.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Player Intent] --> B[Input Device Keyboard Mouse Gamepad]
    B --> C[Input Handler Event Queue]
    C --> D[Game Engine Rule Function R]
    D --> E[World State Vector St]
    E --> F[Renderer Graphics Audio Haptics]
    F --> G[Display Screen Speakers]
    G --> H[Player Perception]
    H --> A
    E -.-> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dashed feedback line from `World State Vector St` back to `Game Engine Rule Function R` represents the recursive nature of the state transition. The player's perception closes the loop by feeding new intent back into the system.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Interactive System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT_LAYER[Input Layer]
        I1[Keyboard Polling]
        I2[Mouse Polling]
        I3[Gamepad Polling]
        I4[Touch Voice Sensors]
    end
    subgraph PROCESSING_LAYER[Processing Layer]
        P1[Input Mapper]
        P2[Rule Engine Rules Scripts]
        P3[AI Subsystem]
        P4[Physics Engine]
    end
    subgraph STATE_LAYER[State Layer]
        S1[World State Vector]
        S2[Entity Component Data]
        S3[Event Queue]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT_LAYER[Output Layer]
        O1[Graphics Renderer]
        O2[Audio Engine]
        O3[Haptic Feedback]
        O4[Network Sync]
    end
    INPUT_LAYER --> PROCESSING_LAYER
    PROCESSING_LAYER --> STATE_LAYER
    STATE_LAYER --> PROCESSING_LAYER
    STATE_LAYER --> OUTPUT_LAYER
    OUTPUT_LAYER --> INPUT_LAYER
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram represents the layered architecture of a real game engine. The *Processing Layer* queries and mutates the *State Layer* every frame, while the *Output Layer* reads the *State Layer* to render. The loop back to the *Input Layer* is what makes the system genuinely interactive rather than merely animated.

## 4.3 Crawford's Three Axes Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Interactivity] --> B[Axis 1 Speed Latency in ms]
    A --> C[Axis 2 Range Variety of Responses]
    A --> D[Axis 3 Mapping Input Output Correlation]
    B --> B1[Below 50 ms feels responsive]
    B --> B2[Above 100 ms feels sluggish]
    C --> C1[Many valid actions = high range]
    C --> C2[Single button = low range]
    D --> D1[Strong mapping feels intuitive]
    D --> D2[Weak mapping feels random]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The three axes are independent but multiplicative. A game with high speed and high range but poor mapping (e.g., controls that do unexpected things) will feel *worse* than a slower but more intuitive game — this is why game designers obsess over input mapping.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define interactivity. List the three axes of interactivity as proposed by Chris Crawford.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
*Interactivity is the real-time bidirectional exchange of information between a player and a game system, in which player input causes a measurable change in game state and produces feedback.* (2 marks)
*The three axes as proposed by Chris Crawford are: **Speed, Range, and Mapping**.* (1 mark)

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is meant by a "game state vector"? Why is it important in defining interactivity?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
*A game state vector is a mathematical representation of all the variables that describe the current condition of a game at a given time **t**, such as player position, score, health, and AI state.* (2 marks)
*It is important because interactivity is formally defined as the transition $S_{t+1} = R(S_t, I_t)$ between successive state vectors, caused by player input — without a defined state, no transition can occur.* (1 mark)

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **Interaction Loop** (Input-Process-Output loop) of a computer game. Describe each stage in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **Chris Crawford's three axes of interactivity** (Speed, Range, Mapping) and explain how each contributes to the *quality* of player experience. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

**Stage 1 — Input Stage (1.5 marks):**
The player expresses intent through physical input devices such as keyboard, mouse, gamepad, touchscreen, or microphone. These devices translate physical actions into digital signals (scan codes, mouse deltas, joystick axes). The raw signals are buffered in an **input event queue**.

**Stage 2 — Processing Stage (2 marks):**
The game engine dequeues the input events, maps them to in-game actions using the **Input Mapper**, and then runs the **Rule Function $R$**. The rule function invokes the AI subsystem, physics engine, and game logic modules. For example, a "fire" input triggers: ammo decrement → projectile spawn → collision check → damage calculation.

**Stage 3 — State Update Stage (1.5 marks):**
The world state vector $S_t$ is mutated to $S_{t+1}$ according to the output of the rule function. All game entities (player, enemies, projectiles, items) have their attributes updated. The new state is written to the entity component database.

**Stage 4 — Output Stage (1 mark):**
The renderer reads the updated state vector and produces a new visual frame. The audio engine plays positional sounds, and the haptic system triggers controller rumble. The result is displayed on the screen, completing the loop.

**Block Diagram (1 mark):** *(Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes for the labelled block diagram.)*

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Axis 1 — Speed (2.5 marks):**
Speed measures the **latency** between player input and system feedback. It is quantified in **milliseconds (ms)**. A latency below 50 ms feels instant; 50–100 ms is acceptable; above 100 ms feels sluggish and breaks immersion. Fast-paced genres (FPS, fighting games) require latency below 20 ms. The *Speed* axis directly determines the *temporal responsiveness* of interactivity.

**Axis 2 — Range (2.5 marks):**
Range measures the **breadth of possible responses** the system can produce. A game with one button has a low range; a game with 100 spells has a high range. However, range must be *meaningful* — padding the range with useless actions does not increase interactivity. *Range* determines the *strategic depth* of interactivity.

**Axis 3 — Mapping (2 marks):**
Mapping measures the **correlation between input and output**. Strong mapping (pressing "jump" makes the character jump) feels intuitive. Weak mapping (pressing "jump" makes the character crouch) feels arbitrary. *Mapping* determines the *clarity* of interactivity.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the **state transition model** of interactivity. Show how the equation $S_{t+1} = R(S_t, I_t)$ is derived and what each term represents. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **interactive and non-interactive media** using the state transition model. Provide two examples for each category. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

The state transition model treats a game as a **discrete-time dynamical system**.

**Term $S_t$ — State Vector (2 marks):**
$S_t$ is a column vector containing all variables that fully describe the game at time $t$. Example: $S_t = [p_x, p_y, p_z, \text{score}, \text{health}]^T$.

**Term $I_t$ — Input Vector (2 marks):**
$I_t$ is the vector of player inputs received during the $t$-th frame. Without $I_t$, the game cannot respond to the player.

**Term $R$ — Rule Function (2 marks):**
$R$ is the deterministic or stochastic function encoding all game rules: physics laws, AI logic, scoring rules, win/lose conditions.

**Equation (1 mark):**
The next state equals the rule function applied to the current state and the player's input: $S_{t+1} = R(S_t, I_t)$.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Non-interactive media** (4 marks):
For non-interactive media, the input term is either absent or ignored, so the state transition degenerates to $S_{t+1} = R(S_t)$ — the next state depends only on the previous state, not on user action.
- *Example 1 — Book:* Each page is a fixed state; the reader cannot alter the plot. $I_t$ is unused.
- *Example 2 — Film:* Each frame is pre-rendered; audience reactions do not change the sequence.

**Interactive media** (3 marks):
For interactive media, $I_t$ is actively consumed by $R$, so $S_{t+1} = R(S_t, I_t)$ — the next state explicitly depends on the player's input.
- *Example 1 — Chess video game:* The board state changes only after the player makes a move.
- *Example 2 — Racing game:* The car's position responds in real-time to steering and acceleration inputs.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "interactivity" with "multimedia"**: A multimedia presentation with clickable buttons is *interactive*, but a slideshow that auto-plays is *not*. Students often lose marks by listing multimedia features instead of defining the bidirectional feedback loop.
> 2. **Forgetting the input term**: When writing $S_{t+1} = R(S_t, I_t)$, students frequently write only $R(S_t)$ — this describes a movie, not a game, and the examiner will deduct marks.
> 3. **Memorizing Crawford's axes incorrectly**: The three axes are **Speed, Range, Mapping** — in that order. Writing "speed, range, and complexity" loses the 1-mark axis-name allocation.
> 4. **Skipping the diagram**: In Part B sub-questions, a labelled block diagram is worth **at least 1 mark** and is often the difference between a 13 and a 14. Always include one.
> 5. **Using "interactivity" and "interaction" interchangeably**: They are related but not identical. *Interaction* is a single event; *interactivity* is the *system property* that enables continuous interactions.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Interactivity** = real-time bidirectional exchange of player ↔ game.
- The **Interaction Loop** has 4 stages: **Input → Process → State Update → Output**.
- The **State Transition Equation** is: $$S_{t+1} = R(S_t, I_t)$$
- **Chris Crawford's Three Axes** = **Speed, Range, Mapping**.
- **Speed** is measured in **milliseconds (ms)**; a good target is **below 50 ms** of latency.
- **Range** = number of meaningful actions/responses the system can produce.
- **Mapping** = strength of correlation between input and the resulting output.
- **60 FPS** target frame rate → $\Delta t = 16.67$ ms per frame.
- The **input term $I_t$** is the mathematical signature that distinguishes an interactive system from a non-interactive one.
- Four types of interactivity: **Player ↔ Game, Player ↔ Player, Game ↔ Game, Player ↔ UI**.
- The **game state vector** $S_t$ must be **fully defined** for any state transition to occur.
- A **block diagram** of the interaction loop is a **high-yield exam artefact** — always draw one when asked.
- The interactive loop is a **closed loop** — feedback must return to the player, otherwise the system is *open-loop* and not truly interactive.
- **Frame time budget** at 60 FPS: input (~1 ms) + process (~8 ms) + render (~7 ms) = 16.67 ms.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multivalent Model of Interactivity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT706 - COMPUTER GAME DESIGN AND PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Multivalent Model of Interactivity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 1: MULTIVALENT MODEL OF INTERACTIVITY

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Multivalent Model of Interactivity** is a player-centric theoretical framework proposed by game scholar **Gordon Calleja (2011)** in his seminal work *"In-Game: From Immersion to Incorporation."* It describes player engagement in digital game environments as occurring through **multiple, simultaneously active, and partially independent channels of involvement**, rather than through a single binary state of "interactivity" (on/off) or a unidimensional immersion gradient (low/high).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Definition (Examiner-Ready):**
> "The Multivalent Model conceptualizes interactivity as a *multivalent* (multi-valued) phenomenon in which the player continuously oscillates between six distinct yet overlapping channels of involvement — **Kinesthetic, Spatial, Shared, Ludic, Narrative, and Affective** — each operating at varying levels of intensity depending on game design, player intent, and contextual factors."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sitting in a **movie theatre watching a 3D IMAX film with surround sound**:
- Your **eyes** (Visual/Spatial channel) follow the scenery.
- Your **ears** (Affective channel) are emotionally stirred by the score.
- Your **hands** (Kinesthetic channel) might grip the armrest during a chase scene.
- Your **mind** (Narrative channel) tries to predict the next plot twist.
- Your **imagination** (Ludic channel) wonders "what if the hero loses?"
- You might lean over and **whisper** to your friend beside you (Shared channel).

You are NOT experiencing *one* thing called "watching a movie." You are experiencing **six overlapping mental states at once**. The Multivalent Model says **gaming is the same** — but even more intense, because you are an **active participant**, not a passive viewer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Multivalent"?**  
> The term comes from chemistry — a *multivalent* atom can form **multiple bonds** simultaneously. Calleja uses this metaphor: a player's mind forms **multiple cognitive/affective bonds** with the game at the same time. No single bond defines the experience; the **synthesis** of all six does.

### 1.3 Why This Model Matters in KTU 2024 Curriculum

- It **moved beyond the flawed binary view** ("game is interactive because the player can press buttons").
- It provides designers with **six explicit design levers** to tune engagement.
- It is the **bridge concept** between Module 1's immersion theories and Module 2/3's practical game design mechanics.

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Interactivity** | The player's capacity to perform meaningful actions that modify game state. |
| **Involvement** | The cognitive/affective investment a player makes in a specific aspect of the game. |
| **Multivalent** | Having multiple simultaneous values or modes of engagement. |
| **Incorporation** | Calleja's higher-order concept where the game becomes "part of" the player's body schema. |
| **Cognitive Bandwidth** | The mental resources available for processing game stimuli (a finite resource split across channels). |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Genesis — From Binary to Multivalent

Earlier models treated interactivity as a single variable:

$$\text{Level of Interactivity} = f(\text{Player Input})$$

Where the function $f$ simply mapped "button pressed" → "game responded." This **Input-Output (I-O) Model** failed to capture *how* the player *experienced* that response.

Calleja's Multivalent Model argues that interactivity has **at least six orthogonal dimensions** of cognitive engagement, and a player's total experience is the **vector sum** (in a conceptual, non-physical sense) of all six:

$$\vec{E}_{\text{player}} = \vec{I}_{\text{kin}} + \vec{I}_{\text{spa}} + \vec{I}_{\text{sha}} + \vec{I}_{\text{lud}} + \vec{I}_{\text{nar}} + \vec{I}_{\text{aff}}$$

Where:
- $\vec{E}_{\text{player}}$ = Total experiential state of the player.
- Each $\vec{I}_x$ = Intensity vector of that specific channel.
- The **direction** of each vector represents *qualitative type* of engagement.
- The **magnitude** represents *intensity* of engagement.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "vector" instead of scalar?**  
> Because engagement is not just "how much" (scalar) but "in what manner" (direction). A player can be highly engaged in *narrative* but disengaged in *ludic* mechanics — and both happen at once.

## 2.2 The Six Channels of Involvement (High-Yield Content)

### 2.2.1 🟦 Channel 1 — Kinesthetic Involvement

- **Definition:** Engagement through **bodily, motor, and tactile actions**. The player's body becomes an instrument of control.
- **Cognitive Focus:** Motor skills, hand-eye coordination, dexterity, reflexes.
- **Design Levers:** Responsive controls, gesture mapping (Wii, VR controllers), haptic feedback.
- **Example:** The "wall-running" feel in *Mirror's Edge*; the precise jumps in *Celeste*.

### 2.2.2 🟩 Channel 2 — Spatial Involvement

- **Definition:** Engagement through **exploration and navigation of the game world's geography**, including learning its layout, landmarks, and boundaries.
- **Cognitive Focus:** Spatial memory, cognitive mapping, orientation.
- **Design Levers:** Non-linear maps, landmarks, mini-maps, environmental storytelling.
- **Example:** Learning the route through *Dark Souls*'s Anor Londo; exploring Hyrule in *Breath of the Wild*.

### 2.2.3 🟧 Channel 3 — Shared Involvement

- **Definition:** Engagement arising from **awareness of other players or game characters** as social entities — both co-present humans and AI/NPCs.
- **Cognitive Focus:** Social cognition, empathy, theory of mind, competition, cooperation.
- **Design Levers:** Multiplayer modes, leaderboards, NPC dialogue trees, guilds.
- **Example:** Coordinating a raid in *World of Warcraft*; feeling guilt in *The Last of Us* (NPC interaction).

### 2.2.4 🟨 Channel 4 — Ludic Involvement

- **Definition:** Engagement through **abstract rule-based problem-solving** — puzzles, strategies, and the formal game mechanics themselves.
- **Cognitive Focus:** Pattern recognition, planning, optimization, rule internalization.
- **Design Levers:** Difficulty curves, puzzle design, meta-strategies, optimal-build theorycrafting.
- **Example:** Solving a *Portal* puzzle; optimizing DPS rotation in *Final Fantasy XIV*.

### 2.2.5 🟥 Channel 5 — Narrative Involvement

- **Definition:** Engagement through **story, plot, character arcs, and the unfolding dramatic sequence**.
- **Cognitive Focus:** Narrative comprehension, suspension of disbelief, empathy with characters.
- **Design Levers:** Cutscenes, environmental narrative, branching dialogue, lore.
- **Example:** The emotional arc of *Red Dead Redemption 2*; piecing together lore in *Hollow Knight*.

### 2.2.6 🟪 Channel 6 — Affective Involvement

- **Definition:** Engagement through **emotional responses** — fear, joy, tension, surprise, attachment, grief.
- **Cognitive Focus:** Affective appraisal, emotional memory, mood regulation.
- **Design Levers:** Music, lighting, pacing, jump scares, music swells.
- **Example:** The "Lavender Town" theme in *Pokémon*; the gut-punch ending of *To the Moon*.

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All key concepts summarized in one table for last-minute revision:**

| Channel | Core Question Answered | Design Trigger | Engagement Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Kinesthetic** | "How do I *physically* do this?" | Responsive controls, haptics | Motor / Bodily |
| **Spatial** | "Where am I, and what is around me?" | Level layout, maps | Exploratory |
| **Shared** | "Who else is here with me?" | Multiplayer, NPC design | Social |
| **Ludic** | "What are the *rules* and how do I win?" | Mechanics, puzzles | Cognitive-Strategic |
| **Narrative** | "What is the *story* and what happens next?" | Scripting, cutscenes | Hermeneutic |
| **Affective** | "How does this *make me feel*?" | Audio, lighting, pacing | Emotional |

## 2.4 The Concept of "Cognitive Bandwidth Reallocation"

Because the human mind has **finite attentional resources**, channels compete for cognitive bandwidth. A shift in one channel can **amplify or suppress** another:

$$\text{Attention}(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{6} A_i(t) \quad \text{where} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{6} A_i(t) \leq A_{\text{max}}$$

- $A_i(t)$ = Attention allocated to channel $i$ at time $t$.
- $A_{\text{max}}$ = Total attentional capacity (a biological constant, roughly Miller's $7 \pm 2$ chunks for short-term memory).

**Engineering Utility:** Game designers use this principle to **layer engagement**:
- During a cutscene → *Narrative* ↑, *Kinesthetic* ↓.
- During a boss fight → *Ludic* ↑, *Affective* ↑ (anxiety), *Spatial* ↑ (dodging).
- During co-op → *Shared* ↑, *Ludic* ↑, *Narrative* ↓ (story takes backseat).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown of the Model

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Construction of the Multivalent Experience

Calleja argues that player engagement follows a **hierarchical progression** through the six channels, ultimately leading to **Incorporation** (his term for the deepest form of engagement where the game becomes part of the player's embodied sense of self).

### Step 1: Initial Sensorimotor Activation (Kinesthetic Onboarding)

The very first thing a player does is **move**. Pressing a stick, clicking a mouse, swiping a screen. This activates the Kinesthetic channel.

$$\text{Player Action} \rightarrow \text{Game Response} \rightarrow \text{Confirmation of Agency}$$

*Why this matters:* Without successful kinesthetic feedback (e.g., a 200ms input lag), the player **fails to even begin** the multivalent experience. Kinesthetic is the **gatekeeper channel**.

### Step 2: Spatial Anchoring (Building the Cognitive Map)

Once the player can move, they **explore**. They form a mental model:

$$M_{\text{world}} = \{L_1, L_2, L_3, \ldots, L_n\}$$

Where each $L_i$ is a **landmark** with attributes: position, accessibility, danger level, resource nodes.

*Why this matters:* A well-designed spatial environment reduces **cognitive load** by providing landmarks, freeing bandwidth for other channels (ludic, narrative).

### Step 3: Rule Internalization (Ludic Mastery)

The player learns the *formal system* — the rules. They build a **mental rulebook**:

$$R_{\text{internal}} = \{r_1, r_2, r_3, \ldots, r_m\}$$

The player tests these rules by experimentation. Each successful test reinforces the rule; each failure refines it. This is the **Ludic loop**.

### Step 4: Narrative Framing (Story Contextualization)

The player contextualizes actions within a story. They ask: *"Why am I doing this?"*

$$N_{\text{arc}} = \text{Setup} \rightarrow \text{Rising Action} \rightarrow \text{Climax} \rightarrow \text{Resolution}$$

A strong narrative **retroactively elevates** prior kinesthetic and ludic actions into meaningful events (e.g., saving a village becomes emotionally charged because the player *knows* the NPCs).

### Step 5: Affective Resonation (Emotional Tagging)

The combined effect of the prior four channels triggers **emotional responses**. The brain tags memories with affect:

$$\text{Memory Strength} \propto \text{Repetition} \times \text{Emotional Intensity}$$

This is why emotionally charged game moments (e.g., the *Red Wedding* of games — Aerith's death in *Final Fantasy VII*) are remembered decades later.

### Step 6: Shared Meaning-Making (Social Layering)

The player **shares** the experience — with online friends, streaming audiences, or even in post-game discussions. This **externalizes** the internal multivalent experience and validates it socially.

### Step 7: Incorporation (Calleja's End-State)

The player internalizes the game such that **virtual elements become part of their extended cognitive schema**. They think in terms of the game's vocabulary even when offline ("I need to 'grind' this report").

## 3.2 Mathematical/Conceptual Mapping of Channel Interdependencies

Calleja's model is not just additive — channels **modulate** each other. A simple 6×6 interaction matrix $C$ can represent this:

$$
C = \begin{pmatrix}
1 & c_{KS} & c_{KSh} & c_{KL} & c_{KN} & c_{KA} \\
c_{SK} & 1 & c_{SSp} & c_{SL} & c_{SN} & c_{SA} \\
c_{ShK} & c_{ShS} & 1 & c_{ShL} & c_{ShN} & c_{ShA} \\
c_{LK} & c_{LS} & c_{LSh} & 1 & c_{LN} & c_{LA} \\
c_{NK} & c_{NS} & c_{NSh} & c_{NL} & 1 & c_{NA} \\
c_{AK} & c_{AS} & c_{ASh} & c_{AL} & c_{AN} & 1
\end{pmatrix}
$$

Where each $c_{ij}$ is a **coupling coefficient** between channels $i$ and $j$, ranging from $-1$ (suppressive) to $+1$ (amplifying).

**Example Read-offs from the Matrix:**
- $c_{KN} > 0$: Kinesthetic actions *amplify* narrative engagement (a sword fight in service of a story feels meaningful).
- $c_{AN} > 0$: Affect *amplifies* narrative memory (sad cutscenes are remembered).
- $c_{LSp} < 0$: Excessive ludic complexity *suppresses* spatial exploration (player gets lost in menus).
- $c_{ShK} > 0$: Shared/social play *amplifies* kinesthetic engagement (multiplayer games feel more "physical").

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:**  
> When asked "How do channels interact?", don't just list them. Use the **modulation/coupling** language. The model is *multivalent* — meaning the channels are **semi-autonomous yet mutually modulating**.

## 3.3 The Transition from "Immersion" to "Incorporation"

A common KTU question is: *"How does the Multivalent Model differ from earlier immersion-based models?"*

The transition is:

$$
\text{Immersion Models (e.g., Brown \& Cairns)}: \text{Engagement} = f(\text{Time}, \text{Familiarity}, \text{Empathy})
$$

$$
\text{Multivalent Model (Calleja)}: \text{Engagement} = \sum_{i=1}^{6} I_i + \sum_{i \neq j} c_{ij} \cdot I_i \cdot I_j
$$

- The first equation is a **single-variable function** (engagement as a scalar).
- The second equation is a **multivariate vector field** with coupling terms — engagement as a **high-dimensional construct**.

This is the **mathematical expression of the conceptual shift** the Multivalent Model represents.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Six Channels and Their Interconnections

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following diagram is a **functional block architecture** showing how the six channels of the Multivalent Model operate in parallel and modulate each other in a real game loop. Render it mentally as a hub-and-spoke with cross-couplings.

```mermaid
graph TD
    PlayerNode["PLAYER CONSCIOUSNESS"]:::core

    subgraph Periphery["MULTIVALENT INVOLVEMENT MATRIX"]
        KinNode["KINESTHETIC INVOLVEMENT\nMotor skills, body control, haptics"]:::kin
        SpaNode["SPATIAL INVOLVEMENT\nExploration, mapping, navigation"]:::spa
        ShNode["SHARED INVOLVEMENT\nMultiplayer, NPCs, social cognition"]:::sha
        LudNode["LUDIC INVOLVEMENT\nRules, puzzles, strategy"]:::lud
        NarNode["NARRATIVE INVOLVEMENT\nStory, plot, character arcs"]:::nar
        AffNode["AFFECTIVE INVOLVEMENT\nEmotion, mood, memory tagging"]:::aff
    end

    OutputNode["INCORPORATION\nGame becomes part of player identity"]:::terminal

    PlayerNode --> KinNode
    PlayerNode --> SpaNode
    PlayerNode --> ShNode
    PlayerNode --> LudNode
    PlayerNode --> NarNode
    PlayerNode --> AffNode

    KinNode -. "amplifies" .-> NarNode
    SpaNode -. "feeds" .-> LudNode
    ShNode -. "amplifies" .-> AffNode
    LudNode -. "contextualizes" .-> NarNode
    NarNode -. "triggers" .-> AffNode
    AffNode -. "reinforces" .-> KinNode
    SpaNode -. "supports" .-> ShNode
    KinNode -. "feeds" .-> SpaNode

    KinNode --> OutputNode
    SpaNode --> OutputNode
    ShNode --> OutputNode
    LudNode --> OutputNode
    NarNode --> OutputNode
    AffNode --> OutputNode

    classDef core fill:#1a1a2e,stroke:#e94560,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef kin fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000000
    classDef spa fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,color:#000000
    classDef sha fill:#ffedd5,stroke:#9a3412,color:#000000
    classDef lud fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#854d0e,color:#000000
    classDef nar fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,color:#000000
    classDef aff fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#5b21b6,color:#000000
    classDef terminal fill:#065f46,stroke:#10b981,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Channel Activation Across Game Phases

```mermaid
graph LR
    StartNode["GAME START"]:::start

    PhaseA["PHASE 1: ONBOARDING\nKinesthetic Dominant"]:::p1
    PhaseB["PHASE 2: EXPLORATION\nSpatial Dominant"]:::p2
    PhaseC["PHASE 3: MASTERY\nLudic Dominant"]:::p3
    PhaseD["PHASE 4: EMOTIONAL PEAK\nNarrative + Affective Dominant"]:::p4
    PhaseE["PHASE 5: SHARING\nShared Dominant"]:::p5
    PhaseF["PHASE 6: INCORPORATION\nAll Channels Integrated"]:::p6

    StartNode --> PhaseA
    PhaseA -->|"Channels shift"| PhaseB
    PhaseB -->|"Channels shift"| PhaseC
    PhaseC -->|"Channels shift"| PhaseD
    PhaseD -->|"Channels shift"| PhaseE
    PhaseE -->|"Final synthesis"| PhaseF

    classDef start fill:#0f172a,stroke:#facc15,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef p1 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000000
    classDef p2 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,color:#000000
    classDef p3 fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#854d0e,color:#000000
    classDef p4 fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,color:#000000
    classDef p5 fill:#ffedd5,stroke:#9a3412,color:#000000
    classDef p6 fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#5b21b6,color:#000000
```

## 4.3 Diagram Interpretation Guide (for Students)

| Diagram Element | Real-World Mapping | KTU Exam Hint |
|---|---|---|
| **Player Consciousness (core)** | The player's mind allocating attention | "Hub of attention" |
| **Six colored nodes** | The 6 channels of the Multivalent Model | Always name **all six** for full marks |
| **Solid arrows (Player → Channel)** | Top-down attention allocation | "Cognitive bandwidth" concept |
| **Dotted arrows (Channel ↔ Channel)** | Mutual modulation / coupling | "Inter-channel influence" |
| **Coupling labels (amplifies, feeds, contextualizes)** | The qualitative nature of inter-channel relation | Use these exact verbs in answers |
| **Incorporation (terminal node)** | Calleja's deepest engagement level | Mention it as the **end-state** |
| **Phase diagram** | How a typical game activates channels in sequence | "Phased engagement" |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the Multivalent Model of Interactivity. Who proposed it and what is its central thesis?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Multivalent Model of Interactivity** was proposed by **Gordon Calleja** in his 2011 work *"In-Game: From Immersion to Incorporation."* Its central thesis is that player engagement in digital games is not a **single, binary, or scalar** state, but rather a **simultaneous, multi-channel experience** occurring across **six distinct but mutually modulating channels of involvement** — *Kinesthetic, Spatial, Shared, Ludic, Narrative, and Affective*. The model rejects the older input-output view of interactivity and instead views the player as continuously oscillating between these six channels, with the **synthesis of all six** producing the phenomenon Calleja calls **Incorporation** — where the game becomes part of the player's embodied self. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for author/source, 1 for the six-channel concept)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**List and briefly define the six channels of involvement in the Multivalent Model.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Channel | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Kinesthetic** | Engagement through bodily movement and motor control of the game (e.g., precise jumps, controller inputs). |
| 2 | **Spatial** | Engagement through exploration and cognitive mapping of the game world's geography. |
| 3 | **Shared** | Engagement arising from the presence of other players or NPCs as social entities. |
| 4 | **Ludic** | Engagement through the rule-based problem-solving and formal mechanics of the game. |
| 5 | **Narrative** | Engagement through the unfolding story, plot, and character arcs. |
| 6 | **Affective** | Engagement through emotional responses to game stimuli (music, lighting, events). |

*(3 marks: ~0.5 marks per channel definition)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **theoretical foundations** of the Multivalent Model of Interactivity. How does it differ from **earlier immersion-based frameworks** such as those of Brown and Cairns? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **six channels of involvement** proposed by Calleja, using **two real game examples per channel** to illustrate how each channel operates in practice. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Answer for Q.A (a):

The Multivalent Model emerged as a **critique of reductionist views** of player engagement. Earlier models, notably **Brown and Cairns (2004)** and the **psychometric immersion scales** of Sherry, treated player engagement as a **single linear variable** that increased over time. Their three-stage model — *engagement → engrossment → total immersion* — assumed a **monotonic increase** in a single dimension.

Calleja identified **two critical flaws** in this approach:

1. **It failed to distinguish types of engagement.** A player can be deeply engaged in *puzzle-solving* (Ludic) but emotionally detached (low Affective), or vice versa.
2. **It treated immersion as binary.** Either you were "in" or "out," with no nuance for the multi-channel nature of experience.

Calleja's **theoretical foundations** rest on three pillars:

- **Phenomenology of play:** Drawing on Husserl and Merleau-Ponty, Calleja argued that play is an *embodied* activity. The body is not just an input device but the *locus* of experience.
- **Cognitive bandwidth theory:** Attention is finite; multiple channels compete for it. The Multivalent Model formalizes this as a **vector sum** of channel intensities.
- **Incorporation as endpoint:** Unlike immersion (which is a temporary state), incorporation describes a **durable, identity-level** change where the game becomes part of the player's extended self.

The key **mathematical/structural difference** is the shift from a **scalar function** of engagement to a **multivariate vector field** with cross-coupling:

$$
E_{\text{old}} = f(t), \quad E_{\text{new}} = \sum_{i} I_i + \sum_{i \neq j} c_{ij} I_i I_j
$$

This shift allows the model to capture **simultaneous and opposing** engagement states (e.g., high ludic + low affective) — something a single-axis model cannot. *(7 marks: 2 for critique of older models, 3 for the three pillars, 2 for the structural shift)*

---

#### ✅ Model Answer for Q.A (b):

| Channel | Game Example 1 | Game Example 2 |
|---|---|---|
| **Kinesthetic** | *Celeste* — precise platforming inputs build motor skill mastery. | *Beat Saber* — VR saber swings are pure kinesthetic engagement. |
| **Spatial** | *The Legend of Zelda: Breath of the Wild* — open-world exploration builds cognitive map. | *Dark Souls* — memorizing Anor Londo's layout is spatial mastery. |
| **Shared** | *Among Us* — social deduction with live players. | *The Last of Us* — emotional NPC relationships (Joel & Ellie). |
| **Ludic** | *Portal* — pure rule-based puzzle engagement. | *Chess.com* — abstract strategy without narrative. |
| **Narrative** | *Red Dead Redemption 2* — Arthur Morgan's arc. | *Hollow Knight* — environmental lore reconstruction. |
| **Affective** | *To the Moon* — emotional journey. | *Limbo* — sustained dread and sadness through visuals/sound. |

In *practice*, channels are **rarely isolated**. In *The Last of Us Part II*, for instance, the player engages:
- **Kinesthetically** during hand-to-hand combat,
- **Spatially** while navigating Seattle,
- **Ludically** through resource management,
- **Narratively** via dual-protagonist structure,
- **Affectively** through grief and moral conflict,
- **Sharedly** through NPC bonds and multiplayer notes.

This is the **essence of multivalent engagement** — a single game scene activates all six channels in a *graded, time-varying* manner. *(7 marks: 1.5 for channel-by-channel explanation, 2 for two examples per channel, 2 for integration of channels in a real game, 1.5 for explaining the multivalent nature)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **"Incorporation"** as proposed by Gordon Calleja. How does it differ from the older concept of "Immersion"? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the **Multivalent Model**, analyze the engagement design of **two contrasting games** — one primarily *narrative-driven* (e.g., *The Witcher 3*) and one primarily *ludic-driven* (e.g., *Tetris Effect*). Show which channels dominate in each. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Answer for Q.B (a):

**Incorporation** is Calleja's term for the **deepest and most durable form** of player engagement, where the game is no longer experienced as an *external* object but becomes **part of the player's embodied self and cognitive schema**. The player thinks in the game's vocabulary, applies its mental models to real life, and retains the game's emotional residue long after stopping.

**Differences from Immersion:**

| Aspect | Immersion (Old View) | Incorporation (Calleja) |
|---|---|---|
| **Duration** | Temporary, in-session | Durable, persists after session |
| **Subjectivity** | Single-axis (in vs. out) | Multi-axis (channels with varying intensity) |
| **Cognition** | Conscious attention focus | Pre-reflective, automatic |
| **Body** | Body is "forgotten" (passive) | Body is *integrated* (active) |
| **Identity** | Self remains distinct from game | Game becomes part of self |
| **Reversibility** | Easily broken by interruption | Resistant to disruption |

Calleja's **six channels progressively feed** into incorporation:
- First, the player **incorporates the controller** (kinesthetic → body schema extension).
- Then the **virtual world** (spatial → cognitive map).
- Then the **rules and narrative** (ludic + narrative → mental models).
- Finally, the **social and emotional residue** (shared + affective → identity layer).

In this sense, incorporation is **not a sudden state** but the **end-product of cumulative multivalent engagement**. *(7 marks: 2 for definition, 3 for comparison table, 2 for progression through channels)*

---

#### ✅ Model Answer for Q.B (b):

#### Game 1: *The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt* (Narrative-Driven)

| Channel | Dominance | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **Kinesthetic** | Medium | Combat involves swordplay and signs, but not the primary focus. |
| **Spatial** | High | Vast open world with rich geographical landmarks. |
| **Shared** | Medium | NPC relationships (Yennefer, Triss, Ciri) carry strong social weight. |
| **Ludic** | Medium | Alchemy and quest mechanics, but not the centerpiece. |
| **Narrative** | **Very High (Dominant)** | Branching storyline, complex characters, moral choices. |
| **Affective** | High | Emotional payoffs in side quests (e.g., *Bloody Baron* arc). |

**Designer Intent:** Channel the player's bandwidth toward *narrative* and *affective* involvement; the other channels support but do not dominate.

#### Game 2: *Tetris Effect* (Ludic-Driven)

| Channel | Dominance | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **Kinesthetic** | High | Rapid piece manipulation. |
| **Spatial** | High | Spatial rotation and fitting pieces. |
| **Shared** | Low | Mostly single-player, though leaderboards exist. |
| **Ludic** | **Very High (Dominant)** | Pure rule-based falling-block puzzle. |
| **Narrative** | Very Low | No explicit story. |
| **Affective** | High (Paradoxical) | Despite no narrative, the music/synesthesia induces strong emotion. |

**Designer Intent:** Channel bandwidth into *kinesthetic + spatial + ludic* — the classic "abstract game" triad. The *affective* channel is engaged through **synesthetic audio-visual coupling** rather than narrative.

**Conclusion:** Both games are **multivalent** (no game engages only one channel), but their **channel-weighting profiles** differ dramatically. This demonstrates the model's power: it can describe *any* game on a **6-dimensional engagement profile**, not just classify them as "narrative" or "ludic" in a binary way. *(7 marks: 2.5 for Witcher 3 analysis, 2.5 for Tetris Effect analysis, 2 for synthesis and model demonstration)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Multivalent Model Questions:**
> 
> 1. ❌ **Listing the 6 channels without explaining inter-channel modulation.** A 14-mark answer that just lists the channels earns ~6–7 marks. You MUST discuss how they *interact*, *modulate*, or *compete for bandwidth*. 
> 
> 2. ❌ **Forgetting to attribute the model.** Always write *"Gordon Calleja (2011), In-Game: From Immersion to Incorporation"* in the first paragraph. Examiners check for this.
> 
> 3. ❌ **Confusing "Immersion" with "Incorporation."** These are **not synonyms**. Immersion is temporary engagement; Incorporation is durable identity-level change. Misusing these terms costs at least 1–2 marks per occurrence.
> 
> 4. ❌ **Treating the model as binary or one-dimensional.** Saying "the player is *immersed* in the game" loses marks. The model is explicitly **multi-dimensional and vector-based**.
> 
> 5. ❌ **Skipping the "why" of channel interaction.** A high-scoring answer explains *why* a strong narrative amplifies affect, or *why* high ludic complexity may suppress spatial exploration (cognitive load argument).
> 
> 6. ❌ **No examples.** Every 14-mark answer needs **at least 2 concrete game examples** with channel analysis. Abstract theory without examples scores ≤ 8/14.
> 
> 7. ❌ **Missing the "multivalent" prefix.** The model is called *multivalent* for a reason — emphasizing **multiplicity of values/channels**. Always foreground this in your answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚡ HIGH-DENSITY RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — MULTIVALENT MODEL OF INTERACTIVITY ⚡**

- 📌 **Author:** Gordon Calleja (2011), in *"In-Game: From Immersion to Incorporation."*

- 📌 **Core Idea:** Interactivity is **multivalent** — the player engages through **six simultaneously active channels** that are partially independent yet mutually modulating.

- 📌 **Six Channels (memorize the order and color them in mind):**
  1. 🟦 **Kinesthetic** — body/motor
  2. 🟩 **Spatial** — exploration/mapping
  3. 🟧 **Shared** — social/NPCs
  4. 🟨 **Ludic** — rules/strategy
  5. 🟥 **Narrative** — story/character
  6. 🟪 **Affective** — emotion/mood

- 📌 **Channels are NOT isolated** — they compete for **finite cognitive bandwidth** and **modulate** each other through coupling coefficients.

- 📌 **Incorporation** is Calleja's **end-state** — the game becomes part of the player's identity, beyond mere immersion.

- 📌 **Mathematical representation:** 
$$\vec{E}_{\text{player}} = \sum_{i=1}^{6} I_i \hat{u}_i + \sum_{i \neq j} c_{ij} I_i I_j \hat{u}_{ij}$$

- 📌 **Difference from earlier models:** Earlier models (Brown & Cairns, Sherry) used a **single-axis / scalar** view. The Multivalent Model uses a **6-dimensional vector** view with **coupling terms**.

- 📌 **Design Implication:** A game designer can **weight the six channels** to create a unique engagement profile — no two games need have the same profile.

- 📌 **Cognitive Bandwidth Limit:** $\sum_{i=1}^{6} A_i(t) \leq A_{\text{max}}$ — the total attention at any moment is bounded.

- 📌 **Common Exam Triggers:**  
  - "Explain the six channels" → List + define + inter-channel modulation.  
  - "Compare with immersion models" → Use the table format.  
  - "Analyze a game using the model" → Use a 6-row channel-dominance table.  
  - "What is Incorporation?" → Differentiate from immersion; explain as end-state.

- 📌 **Real Game Examples to Remember:**  
  - *The Witcher 3* → Narrative-dominant.  
  - *Tetris Effect* → Ludic + Affective (synesthetic).  
  - *Dark Souls* → Kinesthetic + Spatial + Ludic.  
  - *The Last of Us* → All six heavily engaged.  
  - *Among Us* → Shared-dominant.  
  - *Journey* (thatgamecompany) → Affective + Kinesthetic + Spatial.

- 📌 **Examiner Pet-Phrases that Score Marks:**  
  - "Mutually modulating channels"  
  - "Cognitive bandwidth reallocation"  
  - "Vector sum of channel intensities"  
  - "End-state of Incorporation"  
  - "Embodied, phenomenological engagement"

---

**🎯 END OF MODULE 1 NOTES — MULTIVALENT MODEL OF INTERACTIVITY 🎯**

*Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech PECNT706 syllabus, NEP 2020 outcomes, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Levels: Remember → Understand → Apply → Analyze).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interaction and Choice
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECNT756 - COMPUTER GAME DESIGN AND PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Interaction and Choice # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Interaction** in computer game design refers to the bidirectional communication loop established between the **player** and the **game system**, wherein every player input produces a measurable, observable, and rule-governed system response. This loop is the foundational mechanism that transforms a passive medium (like film) into an active, participatory experience. **Choice** is the design mechanism that grants the player meaningful **agency** — the capacity to select between two or more in-game options whose consequences alter the game state, narrative trajectory, or strategic outcome. Choice is the cognitive payload delivered through the interaction loop. In KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education (OBE) language, this topic maps directly to: - **CO1**: *Understand the fundamental principles and architecture of game systems.* - **CO2**: *Apply interaction design principles to construct engaging player experiences.* Mathematically, the Interaction-Choice loop can be expressed as the iterative state transition function: $$ S_{t+1} = f(S_t, I_t, C_t) $$ where $S_t$ is the game state at time $t$, $I_t$ is the player input vector, $C_t$ is the chosen action, and $f$ is the game engine's rule function. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECNT756 Module 1):** Interaction and Choice is the **second pillar** of the foundational triad (alongside *Game Design Fundamentals* and *Game Components*). It is consistently examined as a **Part A (3-mark)** conceptual question and frequently appears as a **7-mark sub-part** within Part B questions on "Player Psychology" or "Game Mechanics." ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Vending Machine vs. Conversation" Distinction Imagine two scenarios: **Scenario A — Vending Machine (Linear Interaction):** You insert a coin ($I_t$), press "B5" ($C_t$), and receive a Coke ($S_{t+1}$). The machine does not remember you, does not adapt, and offers no meaningful choice beyond which button to press. This is **shallow interaction** — present, but devoid of *agency*. **Scenario B — Conversation with a Friend (Deep Interaction):** You ask a question, your friend pauses, considers your tone, your history, and your mood, and responds in a way that *changes based on your choice*. If you had asked differently, the answer would have been different. This is **deep interaction with meaningful choice** — the system adapts to you, and your decisions ripple forward. > [!NOTE] > **The Three Pillars of Meaningful Choice in KTU Framework:** > 1. **Availability** — The player must know that a choice exists. > 2. **Comprehension** — The player must understand the consequences (or believed consequences). > 3. **Significance** — The choice must *materially* affect the game world. If any pillar is missing, the choice is **illusory** — a design anti-pattern formally called **"The Illusion of Choice"** (see Crawford, *Chris Crawford on Game Design*, foundational text for this domain). ## 1.3 Core Terminology Snapshot | Term | Formal Meaning | KTU-Standard Example | |------|----------------|----------------------| | **Input** | Raw signal from hardware device | Mouse click, key press, touch | | **Action** | The mapped game-system response to input | "Jump", "Open Menu", "Fire Weapon" | | **Affordance** | A visual/auditory cue signaling possible interaction | A glowing door, a highlighted button | | **Feedback** | System response confirming the player's action | Sound effect, particle burst, HUD change | | **Agency** | The player's perceived ability to influence the game world | Choosing to spare or kill a boss | | **Ludic Choice** | A choice affecting gameplay mechanics | Selecting a weapon class | | **Narrative Choice** | A choice affecting story progression | Dialogue tree selection | > [!NOTE] > **Constants / Standard Metrics in Interaction Design (industry-recognized thresholds):** > - **Input Latency Threshold:** $\leq$ **16.67 ms** (one frame at 60 FPS) for "imperceptible" lag. > - **Fitts's Law Index of Difficulty (ID):** $ID = \log_2\left(\frac{A}{W} + 1\right)$ — higher ID = harder targeting. > - **Choice Paralysis Threshold:** $\geq$ **7 ± 2** simultaneous options (Miller's Law) before cognitive overload. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Interaction Loop — A Structured Decomposition The interaction loop is not a single event but a **cyclical five-stage process** that repeats dozens of times per second in action games and minutes apart in strategy games. ### Stage 1: **Perception** The player must *perceive* the game state. This involves reading the screen, listening to audio cues, and interpreting HUD elements. Without accurate perception, no choice can be made. - *Why it matters:* KTU examiners frequently test whether students understand that **unseen choices are no choices at all**. - *Design rule:* Always make interactive elements visually distinct using color, lighting, animation, or scale. ### Stage 2: **Cognition (Decision Formation)** The player mentally models possible actions and predicts their outcomes. This is the **"Choice"** stage in its purest form. - *Why it matters:* Choice is fundamentally a *cognitive* event, not a mechanical one. The physical act of pressing a button is merely the *output* of an internal decision process. ### Stage 3: **Input (Action Execution)** The player translates cognition into motor action — pressing a key, clicking a mouse, or tilting a controller. - *Why it matters:* Poor input mapping (e.g., crouch bound to a hard-to-reach key) *destroys* choice regardless of how well-designed the underlying options are. ### Stage 4: **System Processing** The game engine receives the input, validates it against rules, updates internal state variables, and prepares a response. ### Stage 5: **Feedback** The system communicates the result back to the player through visual, auditory, or haptic channels. This closes the loop and begins Stage 1 again. ## 2.2 Taxonomy of Choice in Game Design KTU 2024 examiners reward students who can *classify* choices correctly. The standard taxonomy is: 1. **Trivial / Cosmetic Choice** — Affects appearance but not outcomes. - *Example:* Choosing character skin color. 2. **Strategic Choice** — Affects tactical outcomes within a single encounter. - *Example:* Choosing to attack vs. defend in a turn-based RPG. 3. **Narrative Choice** — Affects story branching. - *Example:* Sparing or killing an NPC in *The Last of Us*. 4. **Meaningful / Consequential Choice** — Permanently alters game state with no rollback. - *Example:* The companion-permanence mechanic in *Fire Emblem*. 5. **Moral / Ethical Choice** — Tests the player's value system. - *Example:* The "Renegade vs. Paragon" axis in *Mass Effect*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Choice Significance Equation (KTU Exam Favorite):** > > $$ > \text{Choice Significance} = P(\text{Outcome Difference}) \times \mid \Delta \text{Utility} \mid > $$ > > where $P(\text{Outcome Difference})$ is the probability that the player perceives the outcomes as different, and $\Delta \text{Utility}$ is the magnitude of state change. A choice is **meaningful** iff this product exceeds a player-defined threshold. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Rule | Application | Units / Notes | |---------|---------------|-------------|---------------| | Game State Transition | $S_{t+1} = f(S_t, I_t, C_t)$ | Core interaction loop | Discrete time steps | | Fitts's Law (Target Acquisition Time) | $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ | UI button sizing, aiming reticles | $MT$ in seconds, $D$ = distance, $W$ = width | | Index of Difficulty | $ID = \log_2\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ | Quantifies targeting difficulty | Dimensionless (bits) | | Hick's Law (Decision Time) | $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(N)$ | Number of menu options | $N$ = number of choices, $T$ in seconds | | Choice Significance | $CS = P(\Delta) \cdot \vert \Delta U \vert$ | Validates "meaningful choice" | $0 \leq CS \leq 1$ (normalized) | | Player Agency Score | $A = \frac{C_{perceived}}{C_{actual}}$ | Measures illusion vs. reality of choice | $0 \leq A \leq 1$; ideal $A = 1$ | | Feedback Latency Budget | $L_{max} \leq 16.67$ ms | Imperceptible input lag | Milliseconds (60 FPS standard) | | Miller's Choice Limit | $N_{opt} \leq 7 \pm 2$ | Maximum simultaneous options | Cognitive psychology rule | | Agency-Difficulty Equilibrium | $\frac{\partial \text{Engagement}}{\partial \text{Challenge}} > 0$ | Flow Channel condition | Csikszentmihalyi's Flow Theory | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The interaction-choice framework is not confined to entertainment. It directly informs: - **UX/UI Engineering:** Mobile app designers use Fitts's Law to size touch targets and Hick's Law to limit menu depth. - **Human-Computer Interaction (HCI):** Industrial control rooms apply the same feedback-loop principles to prevent operator error in nuclear plants. - **Educational Software:** Adaptive learning systems (e.g., Duolingo) build choice trees to personalize pedagogy. - **Automotive UX:** Tesla's touchscreen interface is a battleground of Fitts's Law trade-offs (large targets vs. information density). - **VR/AR Design:** Latency budgets are far stricter (sub-10 ms) to prevent simulator sickness. > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters for KTU examiners:** Questions on Interaction and Choice often include a 2-mark "**real-world application**" component. Citing the above domains signals to the examiner that the student grasps the *transferable engineering value* of the concept, not just its gaming context. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Fitts's Law — Modeling Target Acquisition Time **Problem Context:** A player must click an enemy on a touchscreen. The enemy is at distance $D$ from the player's finger and has on-screen width $W$. Derive the expected movement time $MT$ as a function of $D$ and $W$. **Step 1 — Conceptual Foundation (Shannon-Hartley Analogy):** Paul Fitts (1954) modeled human motor movement as analogous to information transmission. The "difficulty" of acquiring a target is treated as the **information capacity** required to reduce positional uncertainty to zero. $$ ID = \log_2\left(\frac{2A}{W}\right) $$ where $A$ is the amplitude (movement distance) and $W$ is the tolerance (target width). The original formulation used $2A$ to model the worst-case starting position. **Step 2 — Modern Reformulation (MacKenzie, 1992):** The factor of 2 was empirically found to be unnecessary; the standard form used in HCI textbooks today is: $$ ID = \log_2\left(\frac{A}{W} + 1\right) $$ The "+1" prevents the logarithm from going to negative infinity as $W \to \infty$. **Step 3 — Linear Movement Time Model:** Empirically, movement time is linear in information capacity: $$ MT = a + b \cdot ID $$ **Step 4 — Substituting the ID Expression:** $$ MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\left(\frac{A}{W} + 1\right) $$ **Step 5 — Interpretation of Constants:** - $a$ = **reaction/initiation time** (typically 50–200 ms). Represents the base cognitive overhead before movement begins. - $b$ = **empirical slope** (typically 100–160 ms/bit). Represents the rate at which additional target difficulty slows the player. **Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style):** A mobile game button is at distance $A = 200$ px from the player's typical finger rest position and has width $W = 80$ px. Using $a = 100$ ms and $b = 150$ ms/bit, compute $MT$. $$ ID = \log_2\left(\frac{200}{80} + 1\right) = \log_2(3.5) $$ $$ ID \approx 1.807 \text{ bits} $$ $$ MT = 100 + 150 \times 1.807 $$ $$ MT = 100 + 271.05 $$ $$ \boxed{MT \approx 371.05 \text{ ms}} $$ > This means the player takes roughly 371 ms to acquire and tap the button — well within human "instant" perception thresholds. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Hick's Law — Decision Time as a Function of Choices **Problem Context:** A game menu offers $N$ weapon types. Derive the expected decision time $T$ the player takes to select one. **Step 1 — Hick's Original Postulate (1952):** The time to make a choice is proportional to the **information entropy** of the choice set, measured in bits: $$ H = \log_2(N) $$ **Step 2 — Linear Decision Time Model:** $$ T = a + b \cdot H = a + b \cdot \log_2(N) $$ **Step 3 — Engineering Implication:** Every doubling of menu options adds $b$ seconds to the decision time. This is the mathematical justification for the industry practice of **grouping related options** rather than presenting them flatly. **Step 4 — Numerical Example:** A radial weapon wheel has $N = 8$ options. With $a = 100$ ms and $b = 150$ ms: $$ T = 100 + 150 \cdot \log_2(8) = 100 + 150 \cdot 3 $$ $$ \boxed{T = 550 \text{ ms}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Connection to KTU Exam:** A common Part B sub-question asks: *"A game's inventory menu has 64 items shown simultaneously. Using Hick's Law, comment on the usability and suggest a redesign."* The expected answer must reference the logarithmic growth and propose hierarchical grouping (e.g., categories → subcategories → individual items). ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choice Types vs. Player Experience | Choice Type | Cognitive Load | Emotional Weight | Long-Term Memory Encoding | KTU Example Question | |-------------|---------------|------------------|---------------------------|----------------------| | Trivial (Cosmetic) | Low | Negligible | Weak | "Why is character customization not a 'meaningful' choice?" | | Strategic | High | Moderate | Moderate | "How does choice difficulty affect engagement in strategy games?" | | Narrative | Moderate | High | Strong | "Differentiate ludic and narrative choice with examples." | | Consequential | High | High | Very Strong | "Explain the design of a 'point of no return' mechanic." | | Moral/Ethical | Variable | Very High | Strongest | "How do games simulate moral dilemmas without true agency?" | > [!NOTE] > The **strongest memories** in gaming are formed by *moral* and *consequential* choices because they engage both the **System 1** (fast, emotional) and **System 2** (slow, deliberative) cognitive pathways described in Kahneman's dual-process theory. This is why KTU examiners often link Interaction and Choice to player psychology. ## 3.4 Derivation 3: The Agency Equation **Definition:** Player Agency is the ratio of *perceived* choice to *actual* choice: $$ A = \frac{C_{perceived}}{C_{actual}} $$ **Case 1 — Perfect Agency:** $A = 1$ (player sees and acts upon all real choices). **Case 2 — Hidden Agency:** $A < 1$ (game has branching paths, but player is unaware) — design *failure* of communication. **Case 3 — Illusory Agency:** $A \to 0$ (player believes they are choosing, but the outcome is fixed) — the infamous "Mass Effect 3 ending" anti-pattern. **Why it matters:** Many AAA games deliberately use $A < 1$ for narrative coherence. The KTU-acceptable phrasing is that this is acceptable *only if the player never discovers the reduction*. The moment it is discovered, trust is broken and engagement collapses. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Interaction-Choice Feedback Loop ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Player Perception
of Game State"] --> B["Cognitive Decision
Choice Formation"] B --> C["Motor Input
Button/Click/Touch"] C --> D["Game Engine
Rule Validation"] D --> E["State Update
S(t+1) computed"] E --> F["Feedback Layer
Visual + Audio + Haptic"] F --> A style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#BDB76B,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,stroke-width:2px ``` > **Reading the Diagram:** This is a closed-loop system. The five-stage cycle (Perception → Cognition → Input → Engine → Feedback) repeats continuously during gameplay. KTU examiners may ask students to *identify* which stage a specific design failure belongs to (e.g., "Player cannot find the door" = **Stage 1 Perception failure**). ## 4.2 Choice Classification Architecture ```mermaid graph TD R["PLAYER CHOICE
Root Concept"] --> T1["Trivial
Cosmetic Only"] R --> T2["Strategic
Tactical Impact"] R --> T3["Narrative
Story Branching"] R --> T4["Consequential
Permanent State Change"] R --> T5["Moral Ethical
Value System Test"] T1 --> E1["Example: Skin Selection"] T2 --> E2["Example: Weapon Loadout"] T3 --> E3["Example: Dialogue Tree"] T4 --> E4["Example: Companion Death"] T5 --> E5["Example: Spare vs Kill Boss"] style R fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px style T1 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#9370DB style T2 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B style T3 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520 style T4 fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#CD5C5C style T5 fill:#F0FFF0,stroke:#2E8B57 ``` ## 4.3 Input-to-Feedback Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR HW["Hardware Layer
Keyboard / Mouse / Touch"] --> DR["Driver Layer
OS Input Handler"] DR --> EB["Event Bus
Unity Input System
or Unreal Input Mapping"] EB --> AM["Action Mapper
Binds to Game Action"] AM --> GE["Game Engine
State Machine + Rules"] GE --> RP["Renderer Pipeline
Visual Feedback"] GE --> AP["Audio Engine
Sound Feedback"] GE --> HP["Haptic Engine
Controller Rumble"] RP --> P["Player"] AP --> P HP --> P P -.->|New Input| HW style HW fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px style EB fill:#4682B4,stroke:#FFFFFF style GE fill:#32CD32,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px style P fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px ``` > **KTU Examiner Note:** This diagram maps the *technical* counterpart of the *conceptual* loop in Section 4.1. The hardware layer is the *physics*, the engine is the *logic*, and feedback is the *communication* back to the player. ## 4.4 Choice Significance Decision Matrix ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{"Is the outcome
observable to the player?"} Q1 -- "No" --> R1["ILLUSORY CHOICE
Design Anti-pattern"] Q1 -- "Yes" --> Q2{"Is the outcome
permanent or
reversible?"} Q2 -- "Reversible" --> Q3{"Is the choice
tactical or
strategic?"} Q2 -- "Permanent" --> Q4{"Does it affect
morality /
values?"} Q3 -- "Tactical" --> S1["STRATEGIC CHOICE
High replay value"] Q3 -- "Strategic" --> S2["LUDIC CHOICE
Long-term planning"] Q4 -- "No" --> S3["CONSEQUENTIAL CHOICE
Branching narrative"] Q4 -- "Yes" --> S4["MORAL CHOICE
Strongest memory encoding"] style R1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000 style S1 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B style S2 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400 style S3 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082 style S4 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]** *Define "Player Agency" in the context of game design. List and briefly explain the three pillars that determine whether a choice is considered "meaningful."* **Model Answer:** **Player Agency** is the degree to which a player feels their decisions and actions meaningfully influence the game world's state, narrative, or outcome. It is the psychological experience of control, distinct from the *technical* existence of branching options. The **three pillars of meaningful choice** are: 1. **Availability** — The player must be *aware* that a choice exists. Hidden options or non-intuitive interactions do not generate agency. 2. **Comprehension** — The player must understand the *implications* of each option, either through clear UI text, prior experience, or contextual cues. 3. **Significance** — The chosen option must produce a *materially different* game state. Cosmetic-only changes or choices that converge back to a single outcome are not truly meaningful. > *Examiner Note:* Award full 3 marks only if all three pillars are explicitly named and the definition distinguishes *agency* from mere *option count*. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]** *With the help of a suitable example, differentiate between "Ludic Choice" and "Narrative Choice" in game design.* **Model Answer:** | Dimension | Ludic Choice | Narrative Choice | |-----------|-------------|------------------| | **Definition** | A choice affecting gameplay mechanics and systems | A choice affecting story progression and character relationships | | **Scope** | Tactical or strategic | Dramatic or relational | | **Evaluation Metric** | Efficiency, optimization, win probability | Emotional impact, thematic resonance | | **Example** | Choosing between a sword (high damage, slow) and a bow (low damage, ranged) in *Dark Souls* | Deciding to save or sacrifice a companion in *Mass Effect 2*'s suicide mission | | **Replay Driver** | Optimization of build/strategy | Curiosity about alternative plot paths | A player making a **ludic choice** is asking *"What is the optimal action?"* while making a **narrative choice** they ask *"What kind of person do I want to be in this world?"* Both are essential; a game with only ludic choices feels mechanical, while a game with only narrative choices feels like a visual novel. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1 **[KTU University Exam – Module 1 Past Pattern]** **(a)** Explain the **Five-Stage Interaction Loop** in game design. For each stage, provide a concrete design failure example and the corresponding remedy. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Derive Fitts's Law from its information-theoretic foundations. Apply it to compute the expected tap time for a mobile game button located 150 px from the player's resting thumb position with a tap-target width of 60 px. Use $a = 90$ ms, $b = 140$ ms/bit. Comment on whether the design is "comfortable" given that the human "instant response" threshold is 300 ms. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution for (a) — The Five-Stage Interaction Loop:** | Stage | Function | Failure Example | Remedy | |-------|----------|----------------|--------| | 1. Perception | Player reads game state | "Invisible" interactable door with no glow/sound | Add visual affordance: edge-light, particle effect, or NPC hint | | 2. Cognition | Player forms decision | Tutorial overload — too many choices at once | Stagger option introduction; use progressive disclosure | | 3. Input | Player executes action | Critical action mapped to inconvenient key | Remap to ergonomic key; offer key-rebinding | | 4. System Processing | Engine validates and updates | Hit detection misses due to lag | Server-side reconciliation; client prediction | | 5. Feedback | System informs player | Enemy hit produces no visible reaction | Add knockback, hit-flash, blood particle, sound sting | Each stage is *necessary*; a failure in any one breaks the entire loop. The loop is also *cyclic* — the feedback of cycle $n$ becomes the perception of cycle $n+1$. **Model Solution for (b) — Fitts's Law Derivation and Calculation:** **Derivation:** Fitts modeled the human motor system as a noisy communication channel. The **information** required to acquire a target of width $W$ starting from distance $A$ is: $$ ID = \log_2\left(\frac{A}{W} + 1\right) \text{ bits} $$ The "+1" ensures $ID \geq 0$ for $W \to \infty$. Movement time is linear in this information: $$ MT = a + b \cdot ID = a + b \cdot \log_2\left(\frac{A}{W} + 1\right) $$ **Numerical Calculation:** Given $A = 150$ px, $W = 60$ px, $a = 90$ ms, $b = 140$ ms/bit: $$ ID = \log_2\left(\frac{150}{60} + 1\right) = \log_2(3.5) $$ $$ \log_2(3.5) = \frac{\ln(3.5)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{1.2528}{0.6931} \approx 1.807 \text{ bits} $$ $$ MT = 90 + 140 \times 1.807 $$ $$ MT = 90 + 253.0 $$ $$ \boxed{MT \approx 343 \text{ ms}} $$ **Commentary:** Since $MT = 343$ ms $>$ 300 ms, the button is **slightly uncomfortable** for repeated tapping. To improve, the designer should either (i) increase $W$ to $\geq 75$ px, or (ii) move the button closer to the resting thumb position. Recomputing with $W = 75$ px: $$ ID_{new} = \log_2\left(\frac{150}{75} + 1\right) = \log_2(3) \approx 1.585 $$ $$ MT_{new} = 90 + 140 \times 1.585 \approx 311.9 \text{ ms} $$ A larger redesign with $W = 90$ px yields $ID = \log_2(2.667) \approx 1.415$, giving $MT \approx 288$ ms — finally within the "comfortable" threshold. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 **(a)** Discuss the **taxonomy of player choice** in modern game design. For each category, give a published-game example and explain the design rationale. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain **Hick's Law** and apply it to evaluate a radial menu with 12 weapons vs. a hierarchical menu (3 categories × 4 weapons each). Use $a = 100$ ms, $b = 150$ ms. Which design minimizes decision time, and by how much? **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution for (a) — Taxonomy of Player Choice:** 1. **Trivial / Cosmetic Choice** — *Example:* Picking a character skin in *Fortnite*. *Rationale:* Allows personalization without affecting balance, letting players express identity without affecting competitive integrity. 2. **Strategic Choice** — *Example:* Spell selection in *Dota 2* during the drafting phase. *Rationale:* Forces meta-evolution, allows counter-play, and supports competitive depth. 3. **Narrative Choice** — *Example:* The "Tell Tale" dialogue system in *The Walking Dead*. *Rationale:* Creates emotional investment through relational consequences and time-pressure. 4. **Consequential Choice** — *Example:* Permadeath of *XCOM* soldiers. *Rationale:* Transforms abstract units into named individuals, raising emotional stakes and reducing reckless play. 5. **Moral / Ethical Choice** — *Example:* The "Insanity vs. Sanity" duality in *Dishonored*. *Rationale:* Provides narrative depth and high replay value by letting players explore the ethical space of the world. **Model Solution for (b) — Hick's Law Application:** Hick's Law states that decision time grows logarithmically with the number of choices: $$ T = a + b \cdot \log_2(N) $$ **Radial Menu (Flat, $N = 12$):** $$ T_{flat} = 100 + 150 \cdot \log_2(12) $$ $$ \log_2(12) = \log_2(4 \times 3) = 2 + \log_2(3) \approx 2 + 1.585 = 3.585 $$ $$ T_{flat} = 100 + 150 \times 3.585 = 100 + 537.75 = 637.75 \text{ ms} $$ **Hierarchical Menu (Two-Tier, $N_1 = 3$ categories, then $N_2 = 4$ weapons):** Total time is the sum of two sequential decisions: $$ T_{tier1} = 100 + 150 \cdot \log_2(3) = 100 + 150 \times 1.585 = 337.75 \text{ ms} $$ $$ T_{tier2} = 100 + 150 \cdot \log_2(4) = 100 + 150 \times 2 = 400 \text{ ms} $$ $$ T_{hierarchical} = T_{tier1} + T_{tier2} = 337.75 + 400 = 737.75 \text{ ms} $$ **Conclusion:** The **flat radial menu is faster** (637.75 ms vs. 737.75 ms), saving 100 ms — a difference of about **13.5%**. This is because Hick's Law has diminishing returns: $\log_2(12) < \log_2(3) + \log_2(4)$. The flat menu wins on *raw speed*. However, the hierarchical menu is preferred when there are *many* items (e.g., 64+) or when memorization of flat layouts is unrealistic. **Design trade-off:** flat = speed, hierarchical = scalability. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing Interaction with Input.** Interaction is the *full loop*; Input is only Stage 3. Don't conflate them — that's a common 2-mark deduction. > 2. **Forgetting the "+1" in Fitts's Law.** Writing $\log_2(A/W)$ instead of $\log_2(A/W + 1)$ is mathematically valid only when $A \gg W$, but KTU examiners want the correct standard form. > 3. **Ignoring Units.** Always state $MT$ in milliseconds and $ID$ in bits. Bare numbers without units cost a mark. > 4. **Treating "Choice" and "Decision" as Synonyms.** A *decision* is internal; a *choice* is the presented option set. Examiners test this distinction. > 5. **No Real-World Application.** Part B questions on this topic often have a 1-mark "real-world" sub-component.
