# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Explain the role of GLUT in OpenGL programming. List any four core GLUT functions with their syntax.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** GLUT (OpenGL Utility Toolkit) is a window-system-independent library that simplifies the creation of OpenGL applications by providing a portable API for window management, input handling, and event-driven callback registration. It abstracts platform-specific code (Win32, X11, Cocoa) into a single unified interface.

**Core Functions (2 marks):**

1. `void glutInit(int *argc, char **argv);` — Initializes the GLUT library and processes command-line arguments. Must be called before any other GLUT function.
2. `int glutCreateWindow(const char *title);` — Creates a top-level window with the given title and returns a unique window identifier.
3. `void glutDisplayFunc(void (*func)(void));` — Registers a callback that GLUT invokes whenever the window must be redrawn (e.g., after `glutPostRedisplay()`).
4. `void glutMainLoop(void);` — Enters the GLUT event-processing loop. This function **never returns** — all game logic is driven by callbacks registered before this call.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Compare First-Person Shooters (FPS) and Real-Time Strategy (RTS) games on the basis of: (i) camera perspective, (ii) time model, (iii) primary player action.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | FPS | RTS |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| **Camera Perspective** | First-person — rendered from the protagonist's eyes; no avatar visible | Top-down / Isometric — omniscient view of the entire battlefield |
| **Time Model** | Real-time, continuous, with all entities updating every frame (typically 60 FPS) | Real-time, but with macro-level tick updates (e.g., every 50 ms for economy and AI) |
| **Primary Action** | Aim and shoot — focus on reflexes, accuracy, and twitch skills | Build base, gather resources, command units — focus on multitasking and strategic planning |

**Conclusion (½ mark):** While both genres are real-time, FPS emphasizes *individual combat reflex*, whereas RTS emphasizes *large-scale tactical decision-making*.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Option 1)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Describe the architecture of a First-Person Shooter game engine. List its key subsystems and explain the role of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of Turn-Based Strategy games. Discuss their core mechanics and provide two classic examples. Compare TBS with Real-Time Strategy games. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — FPS Engine Architecture (7 Marks)

An FPS engine is a complex real-time system that integrates graphics, physics, AI, audio, and networking. The major subsystems are:

**1. Input Subsystem (1 mark):** Captures keyboard (WASD), mouse (look, shoot), and gamepad events. Maps raw inputs to abstract actions like `MOVE_FORWARD`, `FIRE_PRIMARY`. The mouse delta is converted to camera rotation:
$$\theta_{yaw} += \text{mouse}_x \times \text{sensitivity}$$

**2. Player Controller (1 mark):** Translates abstract actions into physics-impulse vectors. Implements gravity, jumping, crouching, and swimming. The velocity update is:
$$v_{new} = v_{old} + a \times \Delta t$$
$$p_{new} = p_{old} + v_{new} \times \Delta t$$

**3. Physics & Collision System (1 mark):** Uses **AABB (Axis-Aligned Bounding Box)** or **BSP (Binary Space Partitioning)** trees to detect collisions between player, enemies, and world geometry. Responds with sliding-along-wall vectors.

**4. Weapon System (1 mark):** Manages inventory, firing rate (rounds per minute), reload times, and ammo. Each weapon has a state machine: `IDLE → FIRING → RELOADING → IDLE`.

**5. AI Subsystem (1 mark):** Controls enemy behavior using **Finite State Machines (FSM)** or **Behavior Trees**. States include `PATROL`, `CHASE`, `ATTACK`, `FLEE`, `DEAD`. Pathfinding uses **A*** algorithm over a NavMesh.

**6. Rendering Subsystem (1 mark):** Implements the **Graphics Pipeline** — View matrix (camera), Projection matrix (FOV), Lighting (Phong/Blinn shading), Texture mapping, and **Z-buffer** for depth sorting. Targets 60+ FPS.

**7. Audio & Networking (1 mark):** Spatial 3D audio using OpenAL; client-server networking for multiplayer with hit registration and lag compensation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examples:** id Tech (DOOM), Source Engine (Half-Life 2), Unreal Engine (Unreal Tournament).

#### Model Solution for (b) — Turn-Based Strategy Games (7 Marks)

**Definition (2 marks):** A Turn-Based Strategy (TBS) game is a strategy genre where players take **alternating turns** to issue commands. The game clock pauses between turns, allowing deep tactical thought. The world state is **deterministic** — given the same inputs, the same outputs occur, enabling save/reload and replay systems.

**Core Mechanics (3 marks):**

1. **Tile-Based Map:** The world is divided into a grid (hex or square). Each unit occupies one tile and has a *movement range* (e.g., 3 tiles per turn).
2. **Action Economy:** Each unit has a fixed number of action points (AP) per turn. Moving costs AP, attacking costs AP.
3. **Initiative System:** Units act in a defined order (often by a stat like *Speed*). Faster units strike first.
4. **Fog of War:** Mirrors the RTS implementation — unexplored, explored, visible.

**Classic Examples (1 mark):** *Sid Meier's Civilization* (1991), *XCOM: Enemy Unknown* (2012), *Heroes of Might and Magic III* (1999).

**Comparison with RTS (1 mark):**

| Aspect | TBS | RTS |
|--------|-----|-----|
| **Pacing** | Slow, contemplative | Fast, multitasking |
| **Player Skill** | Strategic depth | Reflexes + macro/micro |
| **Pause Feature** | Built-in (game is paused between turns) | Optional (player can pause) |
| **APM Required** | Low (10-30 actions per minute) | High (200+ APM in StarCraft) |

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion:** TBS rewards *thinking*; RTS rewards *thinking + speed*.

---

### Question B (Option 2)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** What are Role-Playing Games? Explain the major character attributes in a typical RPG and derive the formula for computing a character's Hit Points at a given level. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss Multi-User Dungeons (MUDs) in detail. Explain their client-server architecture, common commands, and historical significance as precursors to MMORPGs. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — Role-Playing Games (7 Marks)

**Definition (2 marks):** A Role-Playing Game (RPG) is a genre in which the player assumes the role of one or more characters in a fictional setting, progressing through **character development** (leveling, stats), **narrative** (quests, story arcs), and **exploration**. RPGs are inspired by tabletop games like *Dungeons & Dragons* (1974). The two major sub-genres are **Japanese RPGs (JRPGs)** like *Final Fantasy* and **Western RPGs (WRPGs)** like *The Elder Scrolls*.

**Major Character Attributes (3 marks):** The classical six-attribute system (D&D-derived):

| Attribute | Symbol | Primary Effect |
|-----------|--------|----------------|
| Strength | STR | Melee damage, carry capacity |
| Dexterity | DEX | Ranged accuracy, armor class, initiative |
| Constitution | CON | Hit points, stamina, poison resistance |
| Intelligence | INT | Mana points, spell learning, lore |
| Wisdom | WIS | Willpower, perception, magic resistance |
| Charisma | CHA | NPC disposition, party leadership, trading prices |

**HP Formula Derivation (2 marks):**

Given:
- Base HP at level 1: $\text{HP}_{base}$
- Constitution modifier: $\text{CON}_{mod} = \lfloor (\text{CON} - 10) / 2 \rfloor$
- Per-level HP gain: $d$ (class-dependent, e.g., 6 for Fighter, 4 for Wizard)

**Step 1 — Total level gain:**
$$\Delta_{level} = \text{Level} - 1$$

**Step 2 — HP from leveling:**
$$\text{HP}_{level} = \Delta_{level} \times d$$

**Step 3 — HP from CON modifier (applied each level):**
$$\text{HP}_{CON} = \text{Level} \times \text{CON}_{mod}$$

**Step 4 — Total HP formula:**
$$\boxed{\text{HP}_{total} = \text{HP}_{base} + (\text{Level} - 1) \times d + \text{Level} \times \text{CON}_{mod}}$$

**Example:** A level-5 Fighter with CON 16. Base HP = 10, d = 6.
- $\text{CON}_{mod} = \lfloor (16-10)/2 \rfloor = 3$
- $\text{HP}_{total} = 10 + (5-1) \times 6 + 5 \times 3 = 10 + 24 + 15 = 49$ HP

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — Multi-User Dungeons (7 Marks)

**Definition (1.5 marks):** A **Multi-User Dungeon (MUD)** is a text-based multiplayer online game, typically set in a fantasy world, played over a network using protocols like Telnet. Players connect to a central server, type commands to navigate rooms, fight monsters, and interact with other players. MUDs are the **direct ancestors of modern MMORPGs** like *World of Warcraft* and *EverQuest*.

**Historical Context (1 mark):** The first MUD was created by **Roy Trubshaw and Richard Bartle** at the University of Essex in **1978** (MUD1, also called *Essex MUD*). It ran on a DEC PDP-10 mainframe. Variants followed:
- **DikuMUD** (1991) — C-based, became the basis for *Anarchy Online*, *Aardwolf*.
- **TinyMUD** (1989) — focused on social interaction, no combat.
- **LPMUD** (1989) — used LPC scripting language.

**Client-Server Architecture (2.5 marks):**

```
[Player Terminal] <--TCP/IP, port 4000--> [MUD Server]
                                              |
                                              v
                                    [World Database]
                                    [Player Database]
                                    [Combat Engine]
                                    [Command Parser]
```

- **Client:** A Telnet client (PuTTY, NetRunner) sends text commands; receives text descriptions.
- **Server:** Persistent process running 24/7. Handles:
  1. **Command parsing** — splits input like `kill dragon` into verb + noun.
  2. **World state management** — rooms, items, NPCs.
  3. **Combat resolution** — turn-based, rolling dice via PRNG.
  4. **Inter-player communication** — `say`, `tell`, `shout`, `emote`.
  5. **Persistence** — periodic autosave to disk.

**Common Commands (1 mark):**
- `look` — describe current room.
- `north / south / east / west` — directional movement.
- `kill <target>` — initiate combat.
- `get <item>` / `drop <item>` — inventory management.
- `say <message>` — communicate with room.
- `score` — display character stats.
- `who` — list all online players.

**Significance as MMORPG Precursors (1 mark):** MUDs pioneered the **persistent shared world** concept, **player guilds**, **NPC quest-giving**, and **level-based character progression** — all of which became foundational to graphical MMORPGs in the 2000s. They also demonstrated that text-only interfaces can sustain decade-long player communities (e.g., Aardwolf MUD has been online since 1996).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing GLUT with OpenGL:** GLUT is the *windowing* toolkit; OpenGL is the *graphics* API. Mixing them in definitions costs 1 mark.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the FPS target:** When deriving game loop timing, always mention the target FPS (typically 60). Without it, the derivation is incomplete.
> 
> 3. **RPG stat confusion:** Writing "STR affects mana" loses 1 mark. STR is physical, INT is magical. Know the stat-to-effect mapping.
> 
> 4. **MUD port number:** If asked, the standard MUD port is **4000**, not 80 (HTTP) or 23 (default Telnet). Wrong port = 0.5 mark lost.
> 
> 5. **Puzzle state space:** The 3x3 puzzle has $9!/2 = 181{,}440$ reachable states, not $9!$. Forgetting the parity constraint loses 1 mark.
> 
> 6. **Conflating RTS and TBS:** A common mistake is to call *StarCraft* turn-based. Always specify: RTS = simultaneous real-time, TBS = alternating turns.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist

**GLUT Essentials:**
- [ ] GLUT is a **windowing toolkit**, not a graphics library
- [ ] Always call `glutInit()` first
- [ ] `glutMainLoop()` never returns
- [ ] Timer fires every **16 ms** for 60 FPS
- [ ] Modern alternative: **FreeGLUT** or **GLFW**

**First-Person Shooters:**
- [ ] Camera is **first-person** (player's eyes)
- [ ] Core loop: **input → physics → collision → render**
- [ ] BSP trees used for collision
- [ ] Examples: DOOM, Half-Life, Counter-Strike

**Real-Time Strategy:**
- [ ] **All players act simultaneously**
- [ ] **Fog of War** with 3 states: U, E, V
- [ ] High APM (200+) in competitive play
- [ ] Examples: StarCraft, Age of Empires

**Turn-Based Strategy:**
- [ ] **Alternating turns**, world freezes between
- [ ] **Deterministic** — same input = same output
- [ ] Tile-based grid movement
- [ ] Examples: Civilization, XCOM

**Role-Playing Games:**
- [ ] Six core stats: **STR, DEX, CON, INT, WIS, CHA**
- [ ] Modifier formula: $\lfloor (stat - 10)/2 \rfloor$
- [ ] HP/MP scale with level and primary stat
- [ ] Inspired by **Dungeons & Dragons (1974)**
- [ ] Examples: Final Fantasy, Baldur's Gate, Witcher

**Puzzle Games:**
- [ ] State-space search is the core algorithm
- [ ] 3x3 puzzle: **$9!/2$ reachable states** (parity)
- [ ] 4x4 puzzle: **$16!/2$ reachable states**
- [ ] Examples: Tetris, Portal, Bejeweled

**Multi-User Dungeons:**
- [ ] **Text-based** multiplayer online game
- [ ] First MUD: **MUD1, 1978, Essex University**
- [ ] Default port: **4000** (Telnet-compatible)
- [ ] Architecture: client-server with persistent world
- [ ] Precursor to all MMORPGs
- [ ] Active MUDs still exist (Aardwolf, Discworld)

### 🎯 High-Yield Numerical Formulas to Memorize

$$\text{FPS} = \frac{1}{\Delta t}, \quad \Delta t_{ms} = \frac{1000}{\text{FPS}}$$

$$\text{Stat}_{mod} = \left\lfloor \frac{\text{Stat} - 10}{2} \right\rfloor$$

$$\text{HP}_{total} = \text{HP}_{base} + (\text{Level} - 1) \times d + \text{Level} \times \text{CON}_{mod}$$

$$\text{Damage}_{final} = \text{Damage}_{base} \times (1 - \text{Armor}_{reduce})$$

$$N_{states} = \prod_{i=1}^{k} s_i, \quad N_{reachable} = \frac{N_{states}}{2} \text{ (parity)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** In a 14-mark question, always draw a **diagram** (Mermaid or block diagram) for at least 1 mark. Examiners reward visual clarity. Use the diagrams from Section 4 as templates.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Typical Game Loop - Getting Started with OpenGL Initialization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT756 - COMPUTER GAME DESIGN AND PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Typical Game Loop - Getting Started with OpenGL Initialization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Game Loop** is the central execution engine of any real-time interactive application (game or simulation) that repeatedly processes three fundamental phases — **Input → Update → Render** — at a rate determined by either a fixed timestep or the hardware's vertical synchronization (VSync) signal. It is the heartbeat of a game, decoupling simulation speed from rendering speed.

**OpenGL Initialization** refers to the deterministic sequence of system calls required to bring the GPU into a usable state: creating a native window, establishing a valid **OpenGL Rendering Context**, loading function pointers for the chosen API version, and configuring global pipeline state (depth test, viewport, clear color) before the first draw call can be issued.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 4):** The typical game loop is the foundational structure upon which every subsequent graphics concept (transformations, buffers, shaders) depends. Examiners frequently test the *order* of initialization calls because OpenGL is a **state machine** — an uninitialized state is undefined behavior.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Cinema Projectionist

Imagine a single projectionist (the **CPU/GPU pair**) running a cinema hall. Every single second of the movie, he must perform the same ritual:

1. **Read the script** (Input) — check what the director (player) wants next.
2. **Think about the scene** (Update) — advance the story, move the actors, simulate physics.
3. **Paint the next frame on the canvas** (Render) — send the picture to the screen.

He never stops this ritual until the movie ends. If he paints too slowly, the audience sees stuttering (low FPS). If he paints too quickly without thinking, the movie speeds up uncontrollably (the *"spiral of death"*). The **game loop** is that projectionist's timed ritual, and **OpenGL initialization** is the act of setting up the projector, threading the film, and switching it on *before* the first scene.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **FPS (Frames Per Second)** | The number of complete render cycles executed per second; target is typically **60 FPS** ($\approx 16.67$ ms/frame). |
| **Delta Time ($\Delta t$)** | Real elapsed wall-clock time between two consecutive frames, used to make simulation frame-rate independent. |
| **Rendering Context** | The OpenGL state object that binds OpenGL commands to a specific window on a specific OS. |
| **Double Buffering** | Technique using two framebuffers (front + back) to prevent the user from seeing partially drawn frames (tearing). |
| **VSync (Vertical Sync)** | Hardware signal that caps the frame rate to the monitor's refresh rate (e.g., 60 Hz) to eliminate screen tearing. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in KTU exams?** Almost every Part B question in the Graphics/Game Programming module tests either the *anatomy of the game loop* or the *sequence of OpenGL setup calls*. Memorize the order: `Window Hint → Window Create → Make Context Current → Load Functions → Set Viewport → Start Loop`.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Phase Game Loop Decomposition

A canonical game loop, often called the **Fixed-Variable Game Loop** pattern, executes the following three logical blocks every iteration:

**Phase I — INPUT (Polling Phase)**
- Poll OS event queue (keyboard, mouse, window resize, close button).
- Translate raw OS events into a normalized, game-specific event struct.
- **Why:** Decouples the game logic from the underlying windowing library (GLFW, SDL, Win32).

**Phase II — UPDATE (Simulation Phase)**
- Advance the simulation by a time step $\Delta t$ (real-time delta, not fixed).
- Process AI, physics integration, collision response, animation blending.
- **Why:** Keeping $\Delta t$ variable but *capped* (clamp) prevents the "spiral of death" on slow hardware.

**Phase III — RENDER (Draw Phase)**
- Clear framebuffer(s) (color, depth, stencil).
- Bind shader programs, upload uniform matrices (Model-View-Projection).
- Issue draw calls (`glDrawArrays` / `glDrawElements`).
- Swap front and back buffers.

### 2.2 OpenGL Initialization — The Mandatory Pipeline

For a modern (Core Profile) OpenGL 3.3+ application, initialization **must** follow this exact dependency chain:

1. **Initialize the windowing library** (e.g., `glfwInit()`) — loads platform-specific backends.
2. **Set window hints** (e.g., `glfwWindowHint(GLFW_CONTEXT_VERSION_MAJOR, 4)`) — declares the desired GL version and profile.
3. **Create the window** (`glfwCreateWindow(W, H, "Title", NULL, NULL)`).
4. **Make the OpenGL context current** on that thread (`glfwMakeContextCurrent(window)`) — required before any `gl*` call.
5. **Load function pointers** (e.g., `glewExperimental = GL_TRUE; glewInit();`).
6. **Set the viewport** (`glViewport(0, 0, width, height)`) — defines pixel rectangle for rendering.
7. **Enable required state** (e.g., `glEnable(GL_DEPTH_TEST)`).
8. **Enter the main loop** that calls `glfwPollEvents()`, `update()`, `render()`, `glfwSwapBuffers()`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical rule:** The order of step 4 and step 5 is non-negotiable. Loading function pointers *before* a current context exists will result in a `NULL` function pointer and a hard crash on the first `glClear` or `glDrawArrays` call.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Metrics Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Constant | Units | Engineering Utility |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Target frame budget | $T_{frame} = \frac{1}{FPS}$ | seconds | Caps update/render cost per loop. |
| Delta time | $\Delta t = T_{current} - T_{previous}$ | seconds | Frame-rate independent physics. |
| Monitor VSync cap | $FPS_{max} = f_{refresh}$ | Hz | Eliminates tearing at the cost of input lag. |
| Frame rate measurement | $FPS = \frac{N_{frames}}{T_{elapsed}}$ | fps | Live performance profiling. |
| Resolution independence | $Aspect = \frac{W}{H}$ | ratio | Viewport and projection matrix derivation. |
| Normalized Device Coord (NDC) bounds | $x, y, z \in [-1, 1]$ | unitless | Post-projection clip-space boundary. |
| Perspective projection Y-axis | $f_y = \frac{1}{\tan(\frac{fovY}{2})}$ | unitless | Core of `glm::perspective()`. |

**Engineering Utility of the Game Loop:** In industrial game engines (Unity, Unreal, Godot), the loop is augmented with subsystems — physics fixed timestep, animation tick, audio mixing, network reconciliation — all scheduled inside the Update phase. Understanding this scaffold is essential for engine programmers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivation, Pseudocode & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Frame-Rate Independent Motion

The most critical use of $\Delta t$ in the **Update** phase is to make object velocity independent of the machine's frame rate. If a player moves at $v$ units/second, then the displacement per frame must satisfy:

$$
\Delta \text{position} = v \cdot \Delta t
$$

**Step-by-step derivation (velocity → displacement per frame):**

**Step 1 — Define physical velocity (a constant).**
Let the player velocity be $v = 5$ units/second (a constant scalar in the game world).

**Step 2 — Measure real elapsed time.**
Use a high-resolution timer (e.g., `glfwGetTime()`) to record the timestamp of the current frame $T_n$ and the previous frame $T_{n-1}$.

**Step 3 — Compute the delta time.**
The elapsed wall-clock duration between two consecutive frames is:

$$
\Delta t = T_n - T_{n-1}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the kinematic equation.**
Under uniform motion, the displacement is the integral of velocity over the time interval:

$$
\Delta \text{position} = \int_{T_{n-1}}^{T_n} v \, dt = v \cdot \Delta t
$$

**Step 5 — Update the world coordinate.**
The new position is the previous position plus the displacement:

$$
P_{n} = P_{n-1} + v \cdot \Delta t
$$

**Step 6 — Numerical sanity check.**
If $v = 5$ units/sec and the loop runs at 60 FPS, then $\Delta t \approx 0.01667$ s, so:

$$
\Delta \text{position} = 5 \cdot 0.01667 = 0.0833 \text{ units per frame}
$$

Total displacement over 1 second = $0.0833 \cdot 60 = 5$ units ✓ (matches the defined velocity).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A common student mistake is to update position as $P_n = P_{n-1} + v$ (without $\Delta t$). This makes the game 4× faster on a 240 Hz monitor than on a 60 Hz monitor, and is the classic *spiral of death* bug.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Initialization Call Dependencies

| Order | API Call | Pre-condition | Post-condition / Side-effect |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | `glfwInit()` | None | Windowing subsystem ready; returns `GLFW_TRUE` on success. |
| 2 | `glfwWindowHint(...)` | Library initialized | Sets flags consulted by the *next* `glfwCreateWindow`. |
| 3 | `glfwCreateWindow(W,H,T,M,S)` | Hints set | Allocates OS window + internal GL context (not yet current). |
| 4 | `glfwMakeContextCurrent(w)` | Window valid | Binds the GL context to the calling thread. |
| 5 | `glewInit()` | Context current | Loads all `gl*` function pointers into process address space. |
| 6 | `glViewport(0,0,W,H)` | Context current | Maps NDC $[-1,1]^2$ to the window pixel rectangle. |
| 7 | `glEnable(GL_DEPTH_TEST)` | Context current | Enables per-fragment depth comparison. |
| 8 | `glfwSwapInterval(1)` | Context current | Enables VSync (1 = lock to refresh, 0 = uncapped). |
| 9 | Enter `while (!glfwWindowShouldClose(w))` | Loop body ready | Repeatedly polls, updates, and renders. |
| 10 | `glfwSwapBuffers(w)` | Frame drawn | Swaps back $\leftrightarrow$ front buffer; presents to monitor. |
| 11 | `glfwTerminate()` | Loop exited | Releases all allocated OS resources. |

### 3.3 Reference Pseudocode Skeleton

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following is **structural pseudocode** illustrating the canonical C++ / OpenGL game loop. It is provided for *architectural understanding* consistent with the KTU Computer Game Design syllabus.

```
START PROGRAM
    glfwInit()                                     // (1) initialize windowing lib
    glfwWindowHint(GLFW_CONTEXT_VERSION_MAJOR, 4)  // (2) request OpenGL 4.x
    glfwWindowHint(GLFW_OPENGL_PROFILE, CORE)      //     core profile (no legacy)
    window = glfwCreateWindow(800, 600, "Game")    // (3) create OS window
    glfwMakeContextCurrent(window)                 // (4) bind GL context
    glewInit()                                     // (5) load GL function ptrs
    glViewport(0, 0, 800, 600)                     // (6) set pixel rectangle
    glEnable(GL_DEPTH_TEST)                        // (7) enable z-buffer
    glfwSwapInterval(1)                            // (8) VSync ON

    t_prev = glfwGetTime()                         // (9) initialize clock
    WHILE NOT glfwWindowShouldClose(window):
        t_now   = glfwGetTime()
        delta_t = t_now - t_prev                  // (10) compute dt
        t_prev  = t_now

        glfwPollEvents()                          // (11) PHASE I: INPUT
        processKeyboard()
        processMouse()

        updateSimulation(delta_t)                 // (12) PHASE II: UPDATE

        glClear(COLOR | DEPTH)                    // (13) PHASE III: RENDER
        drawScene()
        glfwSwapBuffers(window)                   // (14) present frame
    END WHILE

    glfwDestroyWindow(window)                      // (15) cleanup
    glfwTerminate()
END PROGRAM
```

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — FPS and Frame Budget

**Given:** A monitor refresh rate of $60$ Hz, a measured average render time of $12$ ms, and a target minimum FPS of $30$.

**Step 1 — Compute the VSync ceiling.**

$$
FPS_{vsync} = f_{refresh} = 60 \text{ fps}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the frame budget under VSync.**

$$
T_{frame} = \frac{1}{60} \approx 0.01667 \text{ s} = 16.67 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute headroom.**

$$
\text{Headroom} = 16.67 - 12 = 4.67 \text{ ms}
$$

The render phase finishes in 12 ms, leaving 4.67 ms of idle GPU time per frame — a healthy engine profile.

**Step 4 — Compute the actual FPS achieved.**

$$
FPS_{actual} = \frac{1}{T_{render} + T_{update}} = \frac{1}{0.012 + 0.002} \approx 71.4 \text{ fps}
$$

Since $71.4 > 60$, VSync will clamp the display to 60 fps; the engine must "sleep" the remaining $\approx 4.67$ ms or risk presenting an identical buffer twice.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Canonical Game Loop — Block Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional flow** of the game loop, including all three phases, the OpenGL initialization prerequisite block, and the resource cleanup teardown block.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Program Entry: main] --> B[Block A: OpenGL Initialization]
    B --> B1[glfwInit - Load windowing lib]
    B1 --> B2[Set window hints - GL version and profile]
    B2 --> B3[glfwCreateWindow - Allocate OS window]
    B3 --> B4[glfwMakeContextCurrent - Bind GL context]
    B4 --> B5[glewInit - Load function pointers]
    B5 --> B6[glViewport and glEnable - Pipeline state]
    B6 --> C{Loop Guard: glfwWindowShouldClose}
    C -- false --> D[Block B: Game Loop Iteration]
    D --> D1[Phase I: INPUT - glfwPollEvents]
    D1 --> D2[Phase II: UPDATE - simulate delta t]
    D2 --> D3[Phase III: RENDER - glClear and draw calls]
    D3 --> D4[glfwSwapBuffers - present frame]
    D4 --> C
    C -- true --> E[Block C: Resource Teardown]
    E --> E1[glfwDestroyWindow]
    E1 --> E2[glfwTerminate]
    E2 --> F[Program Exit: return 0]
```

### 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Per-Frame Data Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as CPU Thread
    participant OS as OS Event Queue
    participant GL as OpenGL Driver
    participant GPU as GPU
    participant MON as Monitor

    CPU->>OS: glfwPollEvents
    OS-->>CPU: keyboard, mouse, resize events
    CPU->>CPU: updateSimulation with delta t
    CPU->>GL: glClear color and depth
    CPU->>GL: glBindVertexArray and glUseProgram
    CPU->>GL: glDrawElements
    GL->>GPU: command buffer submission
    GPU->>GPU: rasterization and shading
    CPU->>GL: glfwSwapBuffers
    GL->>MON: present back buffer at next VSync
    MON-->>CPU: vsync signal approximately 16.67 ms
```

### 4.3 State Machine — OpenGL Context Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Uninitialized
    Uninitialized --> LibLoaded: glfwInit success
    LibLoaded --> Hinted: glfwWindowHint calls
    Hinted --> Windowed: glfwCreateWindow
    Windowed --> Bound: glfwMakeContextCurrent
    Bound --> Ready: glewInit and glViewport
    Ready --> Rendering: enter main loop
    Rendering --> Rendering: glfwSwapBuffers repeats
    Rendering --> Closing: glfwWindowShouldClose true
    Closing --> Destroyed: glfwDestroyWindow
    Destroyed --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams in KTU exams:** Even if the question does not ask for a diagram, drawing a simple 3-box flow (Input $\rightarrow$ Update $\rightarrow$ Render) inside your answer booklet signals high Bloom's *Apply* level mastery and routinely earns full marks in Part B.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]`**
**Define a game loop. List the three fundamental phases of a typical game loop and state the primary purpose of each phase.**
*(3 Marks — Bloom's Level: Remember / Understand)*

**Model Answer:**

A **game loop** is the central control structure of a real-time game that repeatedly processes input, updates the simulation state, and renders the visual output to the display, ensuring continuous interaction and animation.

The three phases are:

1. **Input Phase:** Captures and normalizes user input (keyboard, mouse, gamepad, network events) from the OS event queue and translates it into game-action flags.
2. **Update Phase:** Advances the simulation by a time step $\Delta t$; runs AI, physics integration, collision detection, animation blending, and game-rule logic.
3. **Render Phase:** Issues OpenGL draw calls to clear the framebuffer, upload uniforms, bind shaders, and present the final frame to the monitor via buffer swapping.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2]`**
**Why is the order `glfwMakeContextCurrent()` followed by `glewInit()` mandatory during OpenGL initialization? What happens if the order is reversed?**
*(3 Marks — Bloom's Level: Understand / Apply)*

**Model Answer:**

The `glewInit()` function dynamically loads the addresses of all OpenGL entry points (e.g., `glDrawArrays`, `glCreateShader`) by querying the currently bound OpenGL context. If `glfwMakeContextCurrent()` has *not* been called, no context exists on the calling thread, so GLEW has no context to query and loads `NULL` pointers for every function.

If the order is reversed, the very first call to any `gl*` function (e.g., `glClear`) will dereference a `NULL` function pointer, causing a **segmentation fault** or an **access violation** that terminates the program immediately. Hence the order is mandatory.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

**QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`**

**(a)** Explain the typical game loop architecture in detail. With a neat block diagram, describe the function of the **Input**, **Update**, and **Render** phases, and explain why **delta time ($\Delta t$)** is essential in the Update phase. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Describe the step-by-step procedure to initialize OpenGL using the GLFW and GLEW libraries. Explain the role of `glViewport`, double buffering, and `glfwSwapBuffers` in rendering. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q A (a) — 7 Marks

The **typical game loop** is a `while` construct that iterates as long as the user has not requested a window close. Each iteration executes three sequential phases:

**Step 1 — Input Phase:** The function `glfwPollEvents()` drains the OS event queue. Raw events (`GLFW_KEY_W_PRESS`, mouse-move deltas, window-resize callbacks) are translated into high-level game actions stored in a `InputState` struct (e.g., `input.up = true`).

**Step 2 — Update Phase:** The simulation logic advances using:

$$
\Delta t = t_{current} - t_{previous}
$$

The new world state is computed as:

$$
P_{n} = P_{n-1} + v \cdot \Delta t
$$

Without $\Delta t$, the simulation would run faster on high-refresh-rate monitors, breaking game balance. $\Delta t$ is also **clamped** (e.g., to a maximum of $0.05$ s) to prevent the *spiral of death* if the game stalls (alt-tab, debugger break).

**Step 3 — Render Phase:** The scene is drawn into the back buffer using OpenGL draw calls, then presented to the screen.

**Block Diagram (text rendition for answer booklet):**

```
   +----------------+     +----------------+     +----------------+
   |   INPUT PHASE  | --> |  UPDATE PHASE  | --> |  RENDER PHASE  |
   | glfwPollEvents |     | physics, AI    |     | glClear, draw  |
   |  read keys     |     | delta-t logic  |     | swap buffers   |
   +----------------+     +----------------+     +----------------+
                              ^                          |
                              |_______ frame timing ____|
```

*(Award 3 marks for the three phases, 2 marks for delta-time explanation, 2 marks for the diagram.)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q A (b) — 7 Marks

**Step-by-step OpenGL initialization:**

1. `glfwInit()` — bootstraps the windowing library; **2 marks** for listing 4+ calls correctly.
2. `glfwWindowHint(GLFW_CONTEXT_VERSION_MAJOR, 4)` and `glfwWindowHint(GLFW_OPENGL_PROFILE, GLFW_OPENGL_CORE_PROFILE)` — request a modern core-profile context.
3. `GLFWwindow* window = glfwCreateWindow(800, 600, "Game", NULL, NULL);` — creates the OS window.
4. `glfwMakeContextCurrent(window);` — binds the OpenGL context to the current thread.
5. `glewExperimental = GL_TRUE; glewInit();` — loads function pointers.
6. `glViewport(0, 0, 800, 600);` — maps NDC $[-1, 1]^2$ to the window pixel rectangle.

**Role of `glViewport`:** Defines the rectangular pixel region of the framebuffer where rendering occurs. It is essential after any window resize.

**Role of Double Buffering:** The window owns two framebuffers — *front* (displayed) and *back* (drawing target). The GPU writes into the back buffer; the user only ever sees a fully drawn front buffer, eliminating **screen tearing** and **flicker**.

**Role of `glfwSwapBuffers`:** Atomically swaps the back and front buffers, presenting the freshly drawn frame at the next VSync boundary.

*(Award 2 marks for the initialization sequence, 2 marks for `glViewport` and double buffering, 2 marks for `glfwSwapBuffers` mechanics, 1 mark for neatness.)*

---

**QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]`**

**(a)** What is **delta time ($\Delta t$)** in a game loop? Derive the formula for frame-rate independent position update and show with a numerical example how $\Delta t$ prevents speed inconsistency across machines. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Explain the concept of **VSync (Vertical Synchronization)**. How is `glfwSwapInterval(1)` different from `glfwSwapInterval(0)`? Discuss the trade-offs of enabling VSync in a competitive game. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q B (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition:** Delta time is the wall-clock duration between the start of the current frame and the start of the previous frame, measured in seconds. It is the time quantum consumed by one loop iteration.

**Derivation:**

Let $T_n$ be the timestamp of frame $n$ (returned by `glfwGetTime()`). The elapsed time is:

$$
\Delta t = T_n - T_{n-1}
$$

For uniform motion at velocity $v$, displacement per frame is:

$$
\Delta P = v \cdot \Delta t
$$

The new position is therefore:

$$
P_n = P_{n-1} + v \cdot \Delta t
$$

**Numerical example (KTU-style worked problem):**

Let $v = 10$ units/sec, and suppose two machines run the game — Machine A at 60 FPS and Machine B at 30 FPS.

*On Machine A:* $\Delta t_A = 1/60 \approx 0.01667$ s, so per-frame displacement $= 10 \cdot 0.01667 = 0.1667$ units; total per second $= 0.1667 \cdot 60 = 10$ units ✓.

*On Machine B:* $\Delta t_B = 1/30 \approx 0.03333$ s, so per-frame displacement $= 10 \cdot 0.03333 = 0.3333$ units; total per second $= 0.3333 \cdot 30 = 10$ units ✓.

Both machines traverse **10 units per second**, confirming frame-rate independence.

*(3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for numerical example, 2 marks for conclusion.)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q B (b) — 7 Marks

**VSync (Vertical Synchronization):** A mechanism that synchronizes the presentation of new frames with the monitor's vertical blanking interval, capping the frame rate at the display's refresh rate (typically $60$ Hz).

**`glfwSwapInterval(1)` vs `glfwSwapInterval(0)`:**

| Call | Behavior | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `glfwSwapInterval(1)` | Enables VSync; the driver blocks the `SwapBuffers` call until the next vertical blanking interval. | Cinematic single-player games where smoothness matters more than latency. |
| `glfwSwapInterval(0)` | Disables VSync; `SwapBuffers` returns immediately, allowing the engine to push frames as fast as the GPU can render. | Competitive esports titles (CS2, Valorant) where every millisecond of input lag matters. |

**Trade-offs:**

- **Pro of VSync ON:** Eliminates screen tearing, prevents GPU over-rendering (saves power/heat).
- **Con of VSync ON:** Adds input lag (typically 8–16 ms), and can cause stuttering if the GPU cannot sustain the refresh rate (falls to half-rate).
- **Pro of VSync OFF:** Minimum input lag, highest achievable frame rate.
- **Con of VSync OFF:** Visible screen tearing, higher GPU temperature, possible micro-stutter.

*(2 marks each for explanation, 2 marks for the comparison, 1 mark for trade-offs.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Order of initialization** — writing `glewInit()` *before* `glfwMakeContextCurrent()` — **lose 2 marks instantly**.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention double buffering** when asked about `glfwSwapBuffers` — examiners expect you to state *why* the swap exists (tearing prevention).
> 3. **Confusing FPS with frame time** — a 60 FPS cap means $T_{frame} = 1/60 = 16.67$ ms, not $60$ ms. Many students write the reciprocal incorrectly.
> 4. **Not drawing a diagram** in Part B — even a hand-drawn 3-box flow chart earns 1–2 easy marks in the *presentation* component.
> 5. **Omitting $\Delta t$ clamping** — the *spiral of death* catch is a high-value KTU keyword; missing it costs 1 mark.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Game Loop = Input + Update + Render**, repeated every frame.
- **Initialization order is non-negotiable:** `glfwInit` $\rightarrow$ hints $\rightarrow$ `glfwCreateWindow` $\rightarrow$ `glfwMakeContextCurrent` $\rightarrow$ `glewInit` $\rightarrow$ `glViewport` $\rightarrow$ loop.
- **Delta time $\Delta t$** makes simulation frame-rate independent; always clamp it to avoid the *spiral of death*.
- **Double buffering** is the reason `glfwSwapBuffers` exists; it eliminates tearing.
- **VSync** is controlled via `glfwSwapInterval(0/1)`; trade-off is tearing vs input lag.
- **Core Profile** OpenGL (3.3+) is the KTU-recommended target; legacy fixed-function pipeline is deprecated.
- **Key formulas to memorize:** $T_{frame} = 1/FPS$, $\Delta t = T_n - T_{n-1}$, $P_n = P_{n-1} + v \cdot \Delta t$, $Aspect = W/H$.
- **KTU hot keywords:** *state machine*, *delta time*, *spiral of death*, *vertical sync*, *rendering context*, *function pointer loading*.
- **Anti-pattern to avoid:** Hardcoding per-frame velocity increments ($P_n = P_{n-1} + v$) — this is the #1 cause of *machine-dependent gameplay* bugs.
- **Pro tip for the exam:** If asked "explain the game loop", always start with the *three phases*, then mention *delta time* and *VSync* — this single answer structure typically scores 12–14 out of 14.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Context Types and Window Options - Display Modes, Window Creation, Function Callbacks, Clear Screen, Main Loop, Resizing, Rendering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT756 - COMPUTER GAME DESIGN AND PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Context Types and Window Options - Display Modes, Window Creation, Function Callbacks, Clear Screen, Main Loop, Resizing, Rendering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Rendering Context: The Heart of Graphics Programming

A **Rendering Context** is a data structure that stores all the state information required for an application to interface with a graphics processing unit (GPU). It encapsulates the current OpenGL state, including bound buffers, textures, shaders, viewport dimensions, and transformation matrices. Without a valid rendering context, no graphics command can be executed by the GPU.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A rendering context acts as a "passport" linking the high-level C/C++ application code to the low-level GPU pipeline. Every OpenGL call is bound to the currently active context.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a **rendering context** like a **painter's easel and palette**:
- The **canvas** is the window on your screen
- The **brushes, paints, and tools** represent the OpenGL state (color, line width, shaders, etc.)
- The **painter's hand** is your code issuing draw commands
- The **art studio** is the GPU executing the commands

If you change the canvas (switch windows), you must also remember to bring your paints and brushes (switch the active context). Forgetting this is one of the most common bugs in graphics programming.

## 1.2 The Two Levels of Context in Windows (or X11)

Modern graphics applications use a **two-level context hierarchy**:

| Context Type | Responsibility | Managed By |
|--------------|----------------|------------|
| **Device Context (DC)** | OS-level: drawing surface, resolution, color depth, window handle | Operating System (Win32 GDI / X11) |
| **OpenGL Rendering Context (RC)** | GPU-level: textures, buffers, shaders, current matrices | OpenGL Driver |

On **Windows (Win32)**, you must obtain a DC first (`wglCreateContext`), then bind an OpenGL RC to it (`wglMakeCurrent`). On **Linux/X11**, you use **GLX** (`glXCreateContext`, `glXMakeCurrent`). On **macOS**, NSOpenGLContext handles this transparently.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two contexts?** The OS needs to know *where* to draw (DC), while OpenGL needs to know *how* to draw (RC). They are coupled but distinct.

## 1.3 Display Modes: Windowed vs. Fullscreen

A **Display Mode** defines how the application owns the screen:

- **Windowed Mode**: The application runs inside a window with borders, title bar, and shared screen with other applications. The **window manager** (Explorer, GNOME, macOS Dock) controls placement.
- **Fullscreen Mode**: The application takes exclusive ownership of the display. The window manager is bypassed. Best performance, true vsync, and no taskbar intrusion.
- **Borderless Windowed**: A modern hybrid — a window the size of the screen but without a frame. Common in eSports.

### Conceptual Analogy
**Windowed mode** is like a **food truck** parked in a shared lot — you share the space with other vendors (other apps).  
**Fullscreen mode** is like a **private restaurant** — you rent the whole building for an exclusive experience.  
**Borderless windowed** is like a **drive-through** — the kitchen (fullscreen-like focus) but you can still see neighbors.

## 1.4 Window Creation Libraries: A Comparative View

| Library | Header | Initialization | Main Loop Driver |
|---------|--------|----------------|------------------|
| **GLUT** (legacy) | `<GL/glut.h>` | `glutInit()` | `glutMainLoop()` (blocking) |
| **FreeGLUT** (modern replacement) | `<GL/freeglut.h>` | `glutInit()` | `glutMainLoop()` |
| **GLFW** | `<GLFW/glfw3.h>` | `glfwInit()` | Application-controlled loop |
| **SDL2** | `<SDL2/SDL.h>` | `SDL_Init()` | Application-controlled loop |
| **SFML** | `<SFML/Graphics.hpp>` | `sf::RenderWindow` | Event polling loop |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Trend:** Most new code uses **GLFW** or **SDL2** because they expose the main loop, allowing for better integration with game logic, physics, and AI.

## 1.5 Function Callbacks: Letting the OS Talk Back

A **callback function** is a function pointer that you register with the graphics library, allowing the OS or library to notify your application of events. Without callbacks, your application would need to constantly poll the OS — wasting CPU.

Standard GLUT callback registrations:

- `glutDisplayFunc()` — fires when the window needs redrawing
- `glutReshapeFunc()` — fires when the window is resized
- `glutKeyboardFunc()` — keyboard press
- `glutMouseFunc()` — mouse click
- `glutIdleFunc()` — fires when no other events are pending
- `glutTimerFunc()` — fires after a specified time (ms)

### Conceptual Analogy
A callback is like **registering your phone number at a pizza shop**. You don't stand in line polling "Is it ready yet?" You give them your number; they call you when the pizza (event) is ready.

## 1.6 The Main Loop and Rendering Pipeline

The **Main Loop** is the central heartbeat of every interactive graphics application. Each iteration typically performs:

1. **Process events** (keyboard, mouse, window events)
2. **Update game state** (physics, AI, animations)
3. **Render frame** (clear, draw, swap buffers)
4. **Regulate frame rate** (vsync or timer)

A single iteration is called a **frame**. At **60 FPS**, the loop must complete in **16.67 ms**.

## 1.7 Resizing and the Viewport

When the user drags a window corner, the framebuffer dimensions change. Your code must respond by:
- Updating the **viewport** (`glViewport(0, 0, w, h)`) — the rectangular region of the window where rendering occurs
- Updating the **projection matrix** so geometry doesn't get stretched
- Optionally, recomputing the **aspect ratio**: $\text{aspect} = w / h$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why both viewport and projection?** The viewport tells OpenGL *where* on the window to draw. The projection tells OpenGL *how* to map 3D world coordinates to 2D screen coordinates. Both are needed for correct rendering.

## 1.8 Rendering: From Scene to Pixels

**Rendering** is the process of converting scene descriptions (geometry, textures, lighting) into the final pixel values displayed on screen. It involves:

- **Vertex Processing**: Transformations (model, view, projection), lighting
- **Rasterization**: Converting primitives (triangles) into fragments
- **Fragment Processing**: Per-pixel texturing, depth test, blending
- **Output Merging**: Writing final color to the framebuffer

The **clear screen** step at the start of each frame ensures we don't see artifacts from the previous frame. The **buffer swap** at the end uses **double buffering** to prevent tearing.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an OpenGL Program Lifecycle

Every OpenGL program follows this strict sequence:

| Step | OpenGL Call (GLUT/GLFW) | Purpose |
|------|------------------------|---------|
| 1. Initialize library | `glutInit(&argc, argv)` | Parse CLI args, create internal state |
| 2. Set display mode | `glutInitDisplayMode(GLUT_RGB \| GLUT_DOUBLE \| GLUT_DEPTH)` | Choose color, buffer count, depth/stencil |
| 3. Create window | `glutCreateWindow("Title")` | Open OS window and attach context |
| 4. Initialize OpenGL state | `glEnable(...)`, `glClearColor(...)` | Configure pipeline |
| 5. Register callbacks | `glutDisplayFunc(render)` | Tell library which functions to invoke |
| 6. Enter main loop | `glutMainLoop()` | Begin event dispatching (blocking) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** Steps 1–3 must be in this order. You cannot call `glClearColor` before `glutCreateWindow` because no context exists yet. The calls will silently fail or crash.

## 2.2 Display Mode Flags Explained

GLUT provides bitwise OR-able flags:

- `GLUT_RGB` — 24-bit color (8 bits per channel)
- `GLUT_RGBA` — same as RGB but with alpha channel
- `GLUT_INDEX` — legacy color-indexed mode (avoid)
- `GLUT_DOUBLE` — request double-buffered window (mandatory for animation)
- `GLUT_SINGLE` — single buffer (causes flickering animations)
- `GLUT_DEPTH` — request depth buffer (z-buffer) for 3D
- `GLUT_STENCIL` — request stencil buffer for masking effects
- `GLUT_ACCUM` — accumulation buffer (motion blur, DOF)
- `GLUT_ALPHA` — alpha channel in color buffer
- `GLUT_MULTISAMPLE` — anti-aliasing (FSAA)
- `GLUT_STEREO` — stereoscopic rendering (VR)
- `GLUT_FULLSCREEN` — request fullscreen mode

### Why Double Buffering?

A single-buffered window is shown to the screen immediately. As the GPU draws, you see a half-rendered image — **tearing**. With **double buffering**, drawing occurs to a hidden **back buffer**. When complete, the buffers are swapped in one atomic operation. The user only ever sees a complete frame.

$$T_{\text{frame}} = T_{\text{draw}} + T_{\text{swap}}$$

The swap is typically synchronized to the monitor's refresh rate (**v-sync**), capping your FPS at the monitor's maximum.

## 2.3 Rendering Context Lifecycle on Windows

On Win32 platforms, the sequence is:

1. `hdc = GetDC(hwnd)` — obtain Device Context
2. `PIXELFORMATDESCRIPTOR pfd` — describe desired pixel format (color depth, depth bits, double-buffer)
3. `SetPixelFormat(hdc, ...)` — request that pixel format
4. `hglrc = wglCreateContext(hdc)` — create OpenGL RC
5. `wglMakeCurrent(hdc, hglrc)` — bind RC to DC
6. ... rendering ...
7. `wglMakeCurrent(NULL, NULL)` — unbind
8. `wglDeleteContext(hglrc)` — destroy RC
9. `ReleaseDC(hwnd, hdc)` — release DC

> [!CAUTION]
> Failing to unbind and delete the context causes **resource leaks**. Many game crashes on shutdown are due to improper RC cleanup.

## 2.4 Coordinate Systems and Resize Math

When the window resizes, the **aspect ratio** changes:

$$\text{aspect} = \frac{w}{h}$$

The projection matrix must be updated. For a perspective projection:

$$P = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{1}{\text{aspect} \cdot \tan(f/2)} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \frac{1}{\tan(f/2)} & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \frac{-(f+n)}{f-n} & \frac{-2fn}{f-n} \\ 0 & 0 & -1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

where $f$ is the vertical field of view, $n$ and $f$ are near and far clipping planes. The viewport must also be set:

$$\text{glViewport}(0, 0, w_{\text{new}}, h_{\text{new}})$$

The lower-left corner of the window maps to the lower-left of the NDC cube. Y-axis convention differs by API:
- **OpenGL**: Y increases upward (bottom-left origin)
- **Windows/DirectX**: Y increases downward (top-left origin)

## 2.5 Main Loop Frame Budget Calculation

The relationship between FPS and frame time:

$$T_{\text{frame}} = \frac{1000 \text{ ms}}{FPS}$$

| Target FPS | Frame Budget (ms) | Use Case |
|------------|-------------------|----------|
| 30 | 33.33 | Mobile games, cinematic |
| 60 | 16.67 | Standard desktop/console |
| 120 | 8.33 | High-refresh-rate monitors |
| 144 | 6.94 | eSports competitive |
| 240 | 4.17 | VR (must hit consistently) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For VR (Oculus, HTC Vive), if you drop a frame, the user gets motion sick. The frame budget is **non-negotiable**.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---------|---------|-------|
| Frame time | $T = 1000 / FPS$ | in milliseconds |
| Aspect ratio | $a = w / h$ | width/height of viewport |
| Pixels to NDC | $x_{NDC} = 2x/w - 1$, $y_{NDC} = 2y/h - 1$ | OpenGL convention |
| Vertical FOV to projection scale | $k = \cot(f/2) = 1/\tan(f/2)$ | symmetric frustum |
| Buffer swap delay | $T_{swap} \approx 1/\text{refresh rate}$ | if vsync enabled |
| Depth precision (non-linear) | $z_{NDC} = \frac{(f+n)/(f-n) + 1/z_v}{(f+n)/(f-n) - 1/z_v}$ | near plane has more precision |
| Total pixel count | $N = w \times h$ | governs fillrate cost |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Game Engines (Unity/Unreal)**: Wrap all the above into a `RenderPipeline` class; the main loop becomes the `Update/Render` cycle.
- **Medical Imaging (MRI/CT)**: 3D volume rendering uses perspective projection with custom near/far ranges. Resize handling is critical when doctors zoom.
- **CAD/CAM (AutoCAD, SolidWorks)**: Multi-sample anti-aliasing, double buffering, and stencil buffers for selection highlighting.
- **Aerospace Simulators**: Multiple display modes (mono, stereo, fullscreen dome) require dynamic projection matrix updates.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Aspect Ratio Adjustment on Resize

### Problem Setup
A window originally at **800×600** is resized to **1280×720**. Derive how the projection matrix must change to prevent distortion.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute original aspect ratio.**

$$a_{\text{old}} = \frac{w_{\text{old}}}{h_{\text{old}}} = \frac{800}{600} = \frac{4}{3} \approx 1.333$$

**Step 2 — Compute new aspect ratio.**

$$a_{\text{new}} = \frac{w_{\text{new}}}{h_{\text{new}}} = \frac{1280}{720} = \frac{16}{9} \approx 1.778$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ratio of change.**

$$\Delta a = \frac{a_{\text{new}}}{a_{\text{old}}} = \frac{1.778}{1.333} = 1.333$$

**Step 4 — Apply to projection matrix's $x$-scale term.**

The perspective matrix scales $x$ by $1/(a \cdot \tan(f/2))$. The change factor:

$$\frac{x_{\text{new}}}{x_{\text{old}}} = \frac{a_{\text{old}}}{a_{\text{new}}} = \frac{1}{1.333} = 0.75$$

This means horizontal coordinates are scaled by 0.75× relative to the old projection.

**Step 5 — Update viewport dimensions.**

```c
glViewport(0, 0, 1280, 720);
```

**Step 6 — Rebuild the projection matrix.**

Using `gluPerspective(60.0, a_new, 0.1, 100.0)`, the FOV is preserved at 60°, but the matrix is now correctly built for the 16:9 viewport.

### Verification
Without the update, a square drawn at $(x, y) = (0.5, 0.5)$ in NDC would still render correctly (NDC is normalized). But a square defined in world coordinates would appear stretched horizontally because the $x$-scale term is too small.

## 3.2 Derivation: Frame Time vs FPS Conversion

### Setup
A game engine targets **60 FPS** consistently. Derive the maximum allowed time per frame, accounting for the vsync wait.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Base frame time.**

$$T_{\text{target}} = \frac{1000 \text{ ms}}{60} = 16.667 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 — Subtract vsync wait (1 ms typical).**

$$T_{\text{actual}} = 16.667 - 1.0 = 15.667 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Apply to game logic + render budget.**

If we split 60% to render, 30% to game logic, 10% to safety:

$$T_{\text{render}} = 0.6 \times 15.667 = 9.40 \text{ ms}$$

$$T_{\text{logic}} = 0.3 \times 15.667 = 4.70 \text{ ms}$$

$$T_{\text{safety}} = 0.1 \times 15.667 = 1.57 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4 — Verify sum.**

$$T_{\text{render}} + T_{\text{logic}} + T_{\text{safety}} = 9.40 + 4.70 + 1.57 = 15.67 \text{ ms} \checkmark$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: GLUT vs GLFW Main Loop

| Aspect | GLUT (Legacy) | GLFW (Modern) |
|--------|---------------|---------------|
| Main loop driver | `glutMainLoop()` — internal, no access | User-written `while (!glfwWindowShouldClose(w))` |
| Redraw triggering | Automatic via callbacks | Manual `glfwSwapBuffers` |
| Event polling | Callback-driven only | Polling or callback |
| Multithreaded? | No (single-threaded) | Possible (one window per thread) |
| Game logic integration | Awkward — `glutIdleFunc` | Natural — placed in main loop |
| Frame rate control | `glutTimerFunc` only | `glfwSwapInterval(1)` for vsync, manual `sleep` |
| Time queries | `glutGet(GLUT_ELAPSED_TIME)` | `glfwGetTime()` (double precision) |
| Modern features | None | HiDPI, multi-monitor, raw input, EGL support |
| KTU exam relevance | High (theory) | Medium (practical) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** When asked to "write a main loop" in an exam, the GLUT style is acceptable for short answers, but the GLFW style demonstrates deeper understanding and is preferred for full 14-mark questions.

## 3.4 Derivation: Buffer Swap Timing

### Problem
A 144 Hz monitor runs a game. Calculate the time between successive buffer swaps.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Refresh period.**

$$T_{\text{refresh}} = \frac{1}{144} \text{ s} = 0.00694 \text{ s} = 6.94 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 — If vsync enabled, frame time is locked.**

$$T_{\text{frame}} = T_{\text{refresh}} = 6.94 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum FPS is capped.**

$$FPS_{\max} = 144$$

**Step 4 — If GPU finishes in 3 ms, the remaining 3.94 ms is idle.**

$$T_{\text{idle}} = T_{\text{refresh}} - T_{\text{actual\_work}} = 6.94 - 3.0 = 3.94 \text{ ms}$$

## 3.5 Derivation: Pixel-to-Viewport Mapping

### Problem
A user clicks at screen pixel (640, 360) in a 1280×720 window. Convert to OpenGL Normalized Device Coordinates (NDC).

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Normalize $x$.**

$$x_{\text{ndc}} = \frac{2 \cdot 640}{1280} - 1 = 1.0 - 1.0 = 0.0$$

**Step 2 — Normalize $y$ (OpenGL convention: bottom-left origin).**

$$y_{\text{ndc}} = \frac{2 \cdot 360}{720} - 1 = 1.0 - 1.0 = 0.0$$

**Step 3 — Result.** The click at the center maps to $(0, 0)$ in NDC, the origin of the screen.

**Step 4 — General formula for an arbitrary pixel $(px, py)$:**

$$x_{\text{ndc}} = \frac{2 \cdot px}{w} - 1, \quad y_{\text{ndc}} = \frac{2 \cdot py}{h} - 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> If the OS reports $y$ from the **top** (Windows), you must flip: $y_{\text{ndc}} = 1 - \frac{2 \cdot py}{h}$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 OpenGL Program Initialization Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Program Start] --> B[glutInit arg parsing]
    B --> C[glutInitDisplayMode flags]
    C --> D[glutInitWindowSize Position]
    D --> E[glutCreateWindow creates RC and DC]
    E --> F[OpenGL State Initialization]
    F --> F1[glClearColor]
    F --> F2[glEnable DEPTH_TEST]
    F --> F3[glShadeModel]
    F1 --> G[Register Callbacks]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> G1[glutDisplayFunc]
    G --> G2[glutReshapeFunc]
    G --> G3[glutKeyboardFunc]
    G1 --> H[glutMainLoop]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I[Event Dispatch Begins]
    I --> J{User closes window}
    J -->|No| K[Process Events]
    K --> L[Call Display Callback]
    L --> M[Clear Buffers]
    M --> N[Draw Scene]
    N --> O[Swap Buffers]
    O --> H
    J -->|Yes| P[Cleanup and Exit]
```

## 4.2 Main Loop Frame Architecture

```mermaid

    A[Frame Start] --> B[Process Input Events]
    B --> C[Update Game State]
    C --> D[Physics Simulation]
    C --> E[AI Decision]
    C --> F[Animation Update]
    D --> G[Render Frame]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> G1[glClear]
    G1 --> G2[Update Uniforms]
    G2 --> G3[Bind VAO and VBO]
    G3 --> G4[Issue Draw Call]
    G4 --> G5[Swap Buffers]
    G5 --> H[Frame Rate Regulation]
    H --> I{vsync enabled}
    I -->|Yes| J[Wait for monitor]
    I -->|No| K[Optional sleep]
    J --> A
    K --> A
```

## 4.3 Two-Level Context Architecture (Windows / X11)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Application Code] --> B[OpenGL API Calls]
    B --> C[OpenGL Rendering Context RC]
    C --> D[Bound to]
    D --> E[Device Context DC]
    E --> F[Window HWND]
    F --> G[Operating System]
    G --> H[Window Manager]
    H --> I[Display Hardware]
    I --> J[GPU]
    J --> K[OpenGL Driver]
    K --> C
    style C fill:#FFD700
    style E fill:#87CEEB
    style J fill:#90EE90
```

## 4.4 Callback Event Dispatch Mechanism

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant OS as Operating System
    participant Lib as Graphics Library
    participant App as Application Code
    OS->>Lib: User resizes window
    Lib->>Lib: Detect event in queue
    Lib->>App: Invoke reshape callback w h
    App->>App: glViewport 0 0 w h
    App->>App: Update projection matrix
    App->>Lib: glfwSwapBuffers
    OS->>Lib: User presses key
    Lib->>App: Invoke keyboard callback key x y
    App->>App: Update game state
    OS->>Lib: Vsync signal
    Lib->>App: Allow next frame to draw
```

## 4.5 Resize Handling Pipeline

```mermaid

    A[Window Resize Event] --> B{w and h non zero}
    B -->|No| C[Ignore minimize]
    B -->|Yes| D[glViewport 0 0 w h]

    E --> F[glMatrixMode GL_PROJECTION]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Q: Differentiate between Device Context (DC) and Rendering Context (RC) in OpenGL. Why are both required?**

**Model Answer:**

A **Device Context (DC)** is an OS-level data structure that represents a drawing surface (window, bitmap, or printer). It contains information about the display device — resolution, color depth, and pixel format. On Windows, it is identified by an `HDC` handle; on X11, by a `Display*` pointer.

A **Rendering Context (RC)** is an OpenGL-specific data structure that holds the entire OpenGL state — bound buffers, textures, shaders, transformation matrices, and current color. On Windows, it is identified by an `HGLRC` handle; on X11, by a `GLXContext`.

**Why both are required:**
1. The OS must know *where* to draw (DC), and OpenGL must know *how* to draw (RC). They are separate concerns.
2. The DC can be shared with non-OpenGL drawing (GDI), while the RC is OpenGL-exclusive.
3. On multi-monitor systems, multiple DCs may share one RC, or vice versa, allowing flexible rendering setups.

Both are coupled via `wglMakeCurrent(hdc, hglrc)`, which makes the specified RC active for the specified DC. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - June 2023]**
**Q: What is double buffering? Explain the role of `GLUT_DOUBLE` flag and buffer swapping in animation.**

**Model Answer:**

**Double buffering** is a technique that uses two color buffers — a **front buffer** (visible) and a **back buffer** (offscreen, where drawing occurs). After drawing is complete, the two buffers are swapped atomically, preventing the user from seeing a half-rendered image (known as **tearing**).

**The `GLUT_DOUBLE` flag**, when passed to `glutInitDisplayMode()`, requests that the window be created with double-buffered capability instead of a single buffer. Without it, all drawing appears immediately, causing flickering in animations.

**Buffer swapping** is performed by the call `glutSwapBuffers()`. This function exchanges the front and back buffers in one operation, usually synchronized to the monitor's vertical refresh (vsync) if enabled. The cycle is:

1. `glClear()` — clear the back buffer
2. Draw commands execute to the back buffer
3. `glutSwapBuffers()` — atomic swap
4. Repeat for the next frame

This guarantees a smooth, tear-free animation at the monitor's refresh rate. (3 marks)

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Q (a)** Explain the different display modes available in OpenGL. Discuss the significance of the `glutInitDisplayMode()` function with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The `glutInitDisplayMode()` function in OpenGL specifies the desired properties of the rendering window using a bitwise OR of mode flags. The supported display modes are:

| Flag | Meaning | Use Case |
|------|---------|----------|
| `GLUT_RGB` | 24-bit color | Standard color rendering |
| `GLUT_RGBA` | RGBA color with alpha | Transparency, blending |
| `GLUT_INDEX` | Color-indexed mode | Legacy palette-based graphics |
| `GLUT_SINGLE` | Single buffer | Static scenes, debugging |
| `GLUT_DOUBLE` | Double buffer | Animations, games (mandatory) |
| `GLUT_DEPTH` | Depth buffer (z-buffer) | 3D rendering with hidden-surface removal |
| `GLUT_STENCIL` | Stencil buffer | Masking, shadows, portals |
| `GLUT_ACCUM` | Accumulation buffer | Motion blur, depth of field |
| `GLUT_ALPHA` | Alpha in color buffer | Compositing with desktop |
| `GLUT_MULTISAMPLE` | Anti-aliasing | Smooth edges |
| `GLUT_STEREO` | Stereo rendering | VR, head-mounted displays |
| `GLUT_FULLSCREEN` | Fullscreen mode | Immersive experience |

**Significance:**
- The mode must be set **before** window creation, because it determines the pixel format requested from the OS.
- It is a bitmask, so multiple flags can be combined.
- Once set, it cannot be changed without destroying and recreating the window.

**Example:**
```c
glutInitDisplayMode(GLUT_RGB | GLUT_DOUBLE | GLUT_DEPTH | GLUT_MULTISAMPLE);
```
This requests a 24-bit RGB color window with double buffering, a depth buffer for 3D, and 4× MSAA anti-aliasing — the standard configuration for a modern 3D game. (7 marks)

---

**Q (b)** Describe the role of function callbacks in OpenGL/GLUT. List any five callback functions with their purpose. Write the general structure of a GLUT program. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of Callbacks:**
Function callbacks are user-defined functions whose pointers are registered with GLUT. The library invokes them when specific events occur, allowing the application to react asynchronously. This decouples event detection (handled by GLUT) from event handling (handled by the application), following the **Hollywood Principle** ("Don't call us, we'll call you").

**Five Important Callback Functions:**

| Callback | Purpose |
|----------|---------|
| `glutDisplayFunc()` | Invoked when the window needs redrawing. Contains all `glDraw*` calls. |
| `glutReshapeFunc()` | Invoked on window resize. Updates viewport and projection. |
| `glutKeyboardFunc()` | Invoked on key press. Used for game controls, hotkeys. |
| `glutMouseFunc()` | Invoked on mouse button press/release. Selection, interaction. |
| `glutIdleFunc()` | Invoked continuously when no events are pending. Used for animations. |

**General Structure of a GLUT Program:**
```c
#include <GL/glut.h>

void init(void) {
    glClearColor(0.0, 0.0, 0.0, 1.0);
    glEnable(GL_DEPTH_TEST);
}

void display(void) {
    glClear(GL_COLOR_BUFFER_BIT | GL_DEPTH_BUFFER_BIT);
    // draw scene here
    glutSwapBuffers();
}

void reshape(int w, int h) {
    glViewport(0, 0, w, h);
    glMatrixMode(GL_PROJECTION);
    glLoadIdentity();
    gluPerspective(45.0, (double)w/(double)h, 1.0, 100.0);
    glMatrixMode(GL_MODELVIEW);
}

int main(int argc, char** argv) {
    glutInit(&argc, argv);
    glutInitDisplayMode(GLUT_DOUBLE | GLUT_RGB | GLUT_DEPTH);
    glutInitWindowSize(800, 600);
    glutCreateWindow("KTU OpenGL Demo");
    init();
    glutDisplayFunc(display);
    glutReshapeFunc(reshape);
    glutMainLoop();
    return 0;
}
```
This program opens a window, sets up state, registers callbacks, and enters the event loop. (7 marks)

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**Q (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the main loop of a graphics application. Discuss the significance of the buffer swap and frame rate regulation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**The Main Loop:**
The main loop is the central execution cycle of every interactive graphics application. It runs continuously until the window is closed, executing the following steps each iteration:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start Frame] --> B[Process Events]
    B --> C[Update State]
    C --> D[Render]
    D --> E[Swap Buffers]
    E --> F[Regulate FPS]
    F --> A
```

**Step-by-Step Breakdown:**

1. **Process Events**: Polls or receives OS events (keyboard, mouse, window resize, quit).
2. **Update State**: Advances game logic — physics integration, AI, animation timers.
3. **Render**: Clears buffers, sets up camera, draws all visible objects.
4. **Swap Buffers**: Exchanges front and back buffers in one atomic operation.
5. **Regulate FPS**: Waits for vsync or sleeps to maintain target frame rate.

**Significance of Buffer Swap:**
- Prevents **tearing** (visual artifacts from partial frame display).
- Ensures the user always sees a **complete, consistent frame**.
- Synchronized with **vertical sync** (vsync) to match the monitor's refresh rate.
- Atomic operation — either the new frame is fully shown or the old one.

**Significance of Frame Rate Regulation:**
- Maintains a **consistent user experience** (60 FPS feels smoother than 30 FPS).
- Prevents the GPU from running at 1000 FPS, **saving power** and heat.
- Ensures **deterministic game logic** — physics steps are time-delta-based.
- Avoids **input lag** from irregular frame timing.

The relationship is:
$$T_{\text{frame}} = T_{\text{process}} + T_{\text{update}} + T_{\text{render}} + T_{\text{swap}}$$
For 60 FPS: $T_{\text{frame}} \leq 16.67$ ms. (7 marks)

---

**Q (b)** Explain window resizing in OpenGL. How do you handle a reshape event? Discuss the importance of updating the viewport and projection matrix. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Window Resizing in OpenGL:**
When a user drags the corner of a window, the OS notifies the application. The default behavior of OpenGL is to maintain the previous framebuffer contents, but the **viewport** (the rectangle where rendering occurs) must be explicitly updated, or rendering will be confined to the original dimensions, leaving the new area black or stretched.

**Handling a Reshape Event:**

The reshape callback is registered with `glutReshapeFunc(reshape)`. The function receives the new width and height:

```c
void reshape(int w, int h) {
    if (h == 0) h = 1;  // prevent division by zero
    glViewport(0, 0, w, h);
    
    glMatrixMode(GL_PROJECTION);
    glLoadIdentity();
    gluPerspective(45.0, (double)w/(double)h, 0.1, 100.0);
    
    glMatrixMode(GL_MODELVIEW);
    glLoadIdentity();
}
```

**Importance of Updating the Viewport:**
The `glViewport(x, y, w, h)` call tells OpenGL the rectangular region of the window to render into. The default viewport is the window size at creation. If the window resizes and the viewport is not updated, drawing will be clipped to the old dimensions. The viewport maps NDC coordinates $(-1, 1)$ to the specified pixel rectangle.

**Importance of Updating the Projection Matrix:**
A perspective projection depends on the **aspect ratio** $a = w/h$. If the window changes from 4:3 to 16:9, the previous projection matrix will stretch the image. Rebuilding the projection with the new aspect ratio ensures that:
- Circles remain circles (not ellipses).
- Squares remain squares.
- 3D objects have correct proportions.

**Mathematical relationship:**
$$P_{11} = \frac{1}{a \cdot \tan(f/2)}$$
where $a$ is the new aspect ratio and $f$ is the vertical FOV. When $a$ changes, $P_{11}$ must be recomputed.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** Many students forget the `h == 0` check, causing a **division by zero** crash when the window is minimized. Always include a guard. Additionally, students often forget to call `glutPostRedisplay()` after reshape, which leaves the window showing the old, stretched image until the next user event. (7 marks)

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **Rendering Context (RC)** is a state container; the **Device Context (DC)** is a window handle. They are coupled via `wglMakeCurrent`.
- **Display modes** are bitwise flags passed to `glutInitDisplayMode()` *before* window creation.
- **Double buffering** (`GLUT_DOUBLE`) is mandatory for smooth animation; it uses `glutSwapBuffers()`.
- **Windowed mode** shares the screen; **Fullscreen mode** takes exclusive control of the display.
- **Callbacks** decouple event detection from event handling; common ones are `display`, `reshape`, `keyboard`, `mouse`, `idle`.
- The **main loop** is the heartbeat: process events → update → render → swap → regulate.
- On **resize**: call `glViewport()` and rebuild the **projection matrix** with the new aspect ratio $a = w/h$.
- **V-sync** synchronizes buffer swap to the monitor's refresh rate, preventing tearing.
- **Frame time** $T = 1000/\text{FPS}$: at 60 FPS, the budget is **16.67 ms**.
- **GLUT** uses internal `glutMainLoop()`; **GLFW/SDL2** use user-written `while` loops.
- **Anti-aliasing** requires `GLUT_MULTISAMPLE`; **3D** requires `GLUT_DEPTH`.
- Always guard against `h == 0` in reshape to prevent crashes on window minimize.
- **Double buffering** prevents tearing; **single buffering** flickers.
- The **viewport** specifies *where* to draw; the **projection** specifies *how* to map 3D to 2D.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
