# 🎮 Module 4 — Adding GLEW, Vertices & Shapes in OpenGL

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 GLEW (OpenGL Extension Wrangler)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> *GLEW* is a cross-platform **C/C++ extension loading library** that provides efficient run-time mechanisms for determining which **OpenGL extensions** are supported on the target host platform. It exposes modern OpenGL functionality (e.g., shaders, VBOs, VAOs) that is **not available in the core GL 1.1 API** bundled with most operating systems.

Without GLEW, a programmer using Microsoft's `opengl32.dll` on Windows only gets access to OpenGL 1.1 functions. Modern features like `glGenBuffers`, `glCreateShader`, and `glDrawArrays` (with non-immediate mode) become invisible.

**Conceptual Analogy (The "Translator at a Multilingual Conference"):**
Imagine OpenGL is a **massive library** containing books written in many different versions (1.1, 2.0, 3.3, 4.6, etc.). Your computer's basic GL library only contains the **1.1 bookshelf**. GLEW walks into the library, scans every shelf, and **hands you a universal key** that unlocks **all the modern books** (shaders, buffers, VAOs). The single function `glewExperimental = GL_TRUE;` followed by `glewInit();` is essentially saying: *"Please go fetch every modern book my GPU can read."*

---

### 1.2 Buffer Objects (VBOs / VAOs / EBOs)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Buffer Object** in OpenGL is a contiguous block of **GPU-managed memory** used to store arbitrary vertex data (positions, colours, normals, texture coordinates). The three primary types are:
> - **VBO** — Vertex Buffer Object: stores raw vertex attribute arrays.
> - **VAO** — Vertex Array Object: stores the *configuration* of one or more VBOs (which attributes come from where).
> - **EBO/IBO** — Element (Index) Buffer Object: stores indices that re-use vertices to reduce redundancy.

**Conceptual Analogy (The "Blueprint & Furniture Warehouse"):**
Think of GPU memory as a **massive warehouse**.
- A **VBO** is a **wooden crate** placed in the warehouse. You fill it with the raw coordinates of your game's characters, walls, and trees.
- A **VAO** is a **labelled instruction sheet** taped to the crate. The sheet says: *"Box 1, slot 0 contains X-Y-Z positions; Box 1, slot 1 contains R-G-B colours."* Without the instruction sheet, the GPU has no idea which crate holds what.
- An **EBO** is a **postal index system**. Instead of mailing the *same* letter 6 times, you mail it once and list 6 recipients by index. This shrinks data dramatically.

---

### 1.3 Shaders (GLSL Programs)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Shader** is a small, GPU-executable program written in **GLSL (OpenGL Shading Language)** that operates on vertices, fragments, or geometry. For this module, we focus on:
> - **Vertex Shader** — runs once per vertex; transforms positions.
> - **Fragment Shader** — runs once per pixel; determines colour.

**Conceptual Analogy (The "Painter on an Assembly Line"):**
A vertex shader is an **architect's clerk** who, for every corner of every shape, decides *exactly where* on the screen it should sit. A fragment shader is the **painter** who fills in the millions of pixels inside that shape with colour. Both are tiny C-like programs uploaded to the GPU at runtime.

---

### 1.4 Rectangles via Two Triangles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> OpenGL ES and the modern core profile **do not natively render rectangles**. A rectangle must be constructed from **two triangles** sharing a common diagonal. This is called **triangulation**.

The rectangle in normalised device coordinates (NDC) with corners at $(x_1, y_1)$, $(x_2, y_2)$ has **6 vertices** (2 triangles × 3 vertices). With an **EBO**, the same rectangle can be expressed with only **4 unique vertices + 6 indices**.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The OpenGL Rendering Pipeline (Modern Core Profile)

The data flow for a single rendered frame follows this strict order:

1. **Application Phase (CPU)** — vertices defined in C++ arrays.
2. **VBO Upload** — `glBufferData` copies the array to GPU memory.
3. **VAO Binding** — `glVertexAttribPointer` *records* attribute layout into the currently bound VAO.
4. **Shader Compilation & Linking** — vertex + fragment shaders compiled → linked into a **Program Object**.
5. **Draw Call** — `glDrawArrays` or `glDrawElements` triggers the GPU.
6. **Per-Vertex Stage** — vertex shader runs; outputs `gl_Position`.
7. **Primitive Assembly** — vertices grouped into triangles.
8. **Rasterisation** — triangles converted to fragments (candidates for pixels).
9. **Per-Fragment Stage** — fragment shader outputs `FragColor`.
10. **Tests & Blending** — depth, stencil, blend operations resolve final pixel colour.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — OpenGL Function Cheat Sheet

| Function | Purpose | Key Parameters | Typical Argument |
|---|---|---|---|
| `glewInit()` | Initialise GLEW & load extensions | none | returns `GLEW_OK` on success |
| `glGenBuffers(n, id)` | Generate buffer IDs | `n` count, `id` array | `glGenBuffers(1, &VBO)` |
| `glBindBuffer(target, id)` | Activate a buffer | `GL_ARRAY_BUFFER` / `GL_ELEMENT_ARRAY_BUFFER` | `glBindBuffer(GL_ARRAY_BUFFER, VBO)` |
| `glBufferData(target, size, data, usage)` | Upload CPU data to GPU | `usage`: `GL_STATIC_DRAW` / `GL_DYNAMIC_DRAW` / `GL_STREAM_DRAW` | `glBufferData(GL_ARRAY_BUFFER, sizeof(v), v, GL_STATIC_DRAW)` |
| `glGenVertexArrays(n, id)` | Generate VAO IDs | identical signature to `glGenBuffers` | `glGenVertexArrays(1, &VAO)` |
| `glVertexAttribPointer(idx, size, type, norm, stride, offset)` | Define attribute layout | `stride` = bytes per vertex, `offset` = start offset | pointer offset via `(void*)0` or `(void*)(3*sizeof(float))` |
| `glEnableVertexAttribArray(idx)` | Activate attribute | index matching shader `layout(location=N)` | `glEnableVertexAttribArray(0)` |
| `glCreateShader(type)` | Create shader object | `GL_VERTEX_SHADER` / `GL_FRAGMENT_SHADER` | returns shader ID |
| `glShaderSource(id, count, src, len)` | Upload GLSL source | `src` is `const GLchar* const*` | `glShaderSource(vs, 1, &vsrc, NULL)` |
| `glCompileShader(id)` | Compile a shader | none | check with `glGetShaderiv(..., GL_COMPILE_STATUS, ...)` |
| `glCreateProgram()` | Create program object | none | returns program ID |
| `glLinkProgram(id)` | Link shaders into program | program ID | check with `GL_LINK_STATUS` |
| `glUseProgram(id)` | Activate program for draw calls | program ID | `glUseProgram(shaderProgram)` |
| `glDrawArrays(mode, first, count)` | Draw non-indexed | `GL_TRIANGLES`, starting index, vertex count | `glDrawArrays(GL_TRIANGLES, 0, 3)` |
| `glDrawElements(mode, count, type, offset)` | Draw indexed | `GL_UNSIGNED_INT`, index count | `glDrawElements(GL_TRIANGLES, 6, GL_UNSIGNED_INT, 0)` |

---

### 2.3 Memory-Layout Mathematics for a Vertex

For a vertex with $P = 3$ floats (position) and $C = 3$ floats (colour), the **stride** in bytes is:

$$S_{bytes} = (P + C) \times 4 = 6 \times 4 = 24 \text{ bytes}$$

The **offset** for the colour attribute (which comes *after* the position) is:

$$O_{color} = P \times 4 = 3 \times 4 = 12 \text{ bytes}$$

These values are the **canonical answers** KTU examiners expect in Part B derivation questions.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Vertex Count Optimisation — Indexed vs Non-Indexed Rectangles

**Problem.** Given a rectangle with 4 unique corner positions, how many floats are saved by using an **EBO** versus uploading redundant vertices?

Let the rectangle be defined by 4 corner positions, each requiring $P = 3$ floats (x, y, z). Let the byte size of a float be $b = 4$ bytes.

**Step 1 — Total vertices in the non-indexed approach.**
A rectangle in OpenGL core profile is drawn as two triangles, each with 3 vertices:

$$V_{no\_index} = 2 \times 3 = 6 \text{ vertices}$$

The two triangles share a common diagonal (e.g., vertices 0 and 2), meaning **two vertices are duplicated**.

**Step 2 — Bytes uploaded without EBO.**

$$B_{no\_index} = V_{no\_index} \times P \times b = 6 \times 3 \times 4 = 72 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 3 — Vertices with EBO.**
Only the unique corners are uploaded:

$$V_{with\_index} = 4 \text{ vertices}$$

**Step 4 — Bytes for position data with EBO.**

$$B_{pos} = V_{with\_index} \times P \times b = 4 \times 3 \times 4 = 48 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 5 — Index buffer size.**
Indices are typically `GL_UNSIGNED_INT` (4 bytes):

$$B_{idx} = 6 \times 4 = 24 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 6 — Total bytes uploaded with EBO.**

$$B_{with\_index} = B_{pos} + B_{idx} = 48 + 24 = 72 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 7 — Net savings.**
At first glance both approaches are equal. The savings appear *only* as **vertex shader invocations** are reduced from 6 to 4 (a 33.3% reduction). For attributes like normals, UVs, and tangents (each 3+ floats per vertex), the savings multiply rapidly. For a cube ($8$ unique vertices, $36$ indexed draws), or a UV-sphere ($N$ segments), the savings become substantial.

For $K$ extra attributes of $A$ floats each, the **vertex-shader-invocation savings** in bytes (per draw) are:

$$\Delta B = (V_{no\_index} - V_{with\_index}) \times A \times b = 2 \times A \times 4 = 8A \text{ bytes}$$

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: GLEW Initialisation Block

```cpp
#define GLEW_STATIC
#include <GL/glew.h>
#include <GLFW/glfw3.h>

int main() {
    if (!glfwInit()) return -1;
    GLFWwindow* window = glfwCreateWindow(800, 600, "KTU OpenGL", NULL, NULL);
    glfwMakeContextCurrent(window);

    glewExperimental = GL_TRUE;             // (1) Enable modern extension exposure
    if (glewInit() != GLEW_OK) return -1;   // (2) Load all supported extensions

    // (3) Compile vertex shader
    const GLchar* vShaderSrc =
        "#version 330 core\n"
        "layout (location = 0) in vec3 aPos;\n"
        "void main() { gl_Position = vec4(aPos, 1.0); }";
    GLuint vs = glCreateShader(GL_VERTEX_SHADER);
    glShaderSource(vs, 1, &vShaderSrc, NULL);
    glCompileShader(vs);

    // (4) Compile fragment shader
    const GLchar* fShaderSrc =
        "#version 330 core\n"
        "out vec4 FragColor;\n"
        "void main() { FragColor = vec4(0.2f, 0.7f, 0.3f, 1.0f); }";
    GLuint fs = glCreateShader(GL_FRAGMENT_SHADER);
    glShaderSource(fs, 1, &fShaderSrc, NULL);
    glCompileShader(fs);

    // (5) Link program
    GLuint prog = glCreateProgram();
    glAttachShader(prog, vs); glAttachShader(prog, fs);
    glLinkProgram(prog);

    // (6) Define rectangle vertices (2 triangles)
    GLfloat verts[] = {
         0.5f,  0.5f, 0.0f,   // top-right   (idx 0)
         0.5f, -0.5f, 0.0f,   // bottom-right(idx 1)
        -0.5f, -0.5f, 0.0f,   // bottom-left (idx 2)
        -0.5f,  0.5f, 0.0f    // top-left    (idx 3)
    };
    GLuint idx[] = { 0, 1, 3, 1, 2, 3 };

    // (7) Generate + bind VAO, then VBO + EBO
    GLuint VAO, VBO, EBO;
    glGenVertexArrays(1, &VAO);
    glGenBuffers(1, &VBO);
    glGenBuffers(1, &EBO);
    glBindVertexArray(VAO);
    glBindBuffer(GL_ARRAY_BUFFER, VBO);
    glBufferData(GL_ARRAY_BUFFER, sizeof(verts), verts, GL_STATIC_DRAW);
    glBindBuffer(GL_ELEMENT_ARRAY_BUFFER, EBO);
    glBufferData(GL_ELEMENT_ARRAY_BUFFER, sizeof(idx), idx, GL_STATIC_DRAW);

    // (8) Attribute pointer for aPos (location = 0)
    glVertexAttribPointer(0, 3, GL_FLOAT, GL_FALSE, 3*sizeof(GLfloat), (void*)0);
    glEnableVertexAttribArray(0);
    glBindBuffer(GL_ARRAY_BUFFER, 0);
    glBindVertexArray(0);

    // (9) Render loop
    while (!glfwWindowShouldClose(window)) {
        glfwPollEvents();
        glClearColor(0.1f, 0.1f, 0.15f, 1.0f);
        glClear(GL_COLOR_BUFFER_BIT);
        glUseProgram(prog);
        glBindVertexArray(VAO);
        glDrawElements(GL_TRIANGLES, 6, GL_UNSIGNED_INT, 0);
        glfwSwapBuffers(window);
    }
    glfwTerminate();
    return 0;
}
```

---

### 3.3 Analytical Walk-Through Table

| Line / Block | OpenGL State Change | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| `glewExperimental = GL_TRUE` | Tells GLEW to expose non-core (modern) functions | Without this, `glGenBuffers` may return `NULL` on some drivers |
| `glewInit()` | Populates function pointer table | Must be called *after* a GL context is current |
| `glCreateShader(...)` | Allocates GPU-side shader object | Each shader type needs its own object |
| `glShaderSource(...)` | Copies GLSL string into shader object | Source is *copied*; can free host string after |
| `glCompileShader(...)` | Invokes GLSL compiler on GPU driver | Always check `GL_COMPILE_STATUS` |
| `glLinkProgram(...)` | Combiles VS+FS into executable pipeline | Linking failures ≠ compilation failures |
| `glGenVertexArrays(1,&VAO)` | Creates empty VAO descriptor | Required in core profile ≥ 3.0 |
| `glBindVertexArray(VAO)` | Activates VAO for recording | All subsequent `glVertexAttribPointer` calls are stored in it |
| `glBufferData(..., GL_STATIC_DRAW)` | Hints GPU: data uploaded once, drawn many times | Helps driver pick optimal memory (VRAM vs GART) |
| `glVertexAttribPointer(0,3,...)` | Tells GPU: "attribute 0 = vec3 from this VBO" | `location` must match shader's `layout(location=0)` |
| `glDrawElements(...,6,...)` | Renders 6 indices → 2 triangles → 1 rectangle | Triggers full pipeline execution |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid: Modern OpenGL Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CPU Application Code] --> B[Define Vertex Array in C++]
    B --> C[glGenBuffers - Allocate VBO]
    C --> D[glBindBuffer - Activate VBO]
    D --> E[glBufferData - Upload Vertices to GPU]
    E --> F[glGenVertexArrays - Allocate VAO]
    F --> G[glBindVertexArray - Activate VAO]
    G --> H[glVertexAttribPointer - Define Attribute Layout]
    H --> I[glEnableVertexAttribArray - Activate Attribute]
    I --> J[glCreateShader - Vertex Shader]
    J --> K[glCompileShader - Compile GLSL]
    K --> L[glCreateShader - Fragment Shader]
    L --> M[glCompileShader - Compile GLSL]
    M --> N[glCreateProgram - Allocate Program]
    N --> O[glAttachShader x2]
    O --> P[glLinkProgram - Link Shaders]
    P --> Q[glUseProgram - Activate Program]
    Q --> R[glBindVertexArray - Bind Geometry]
    R --> S[glDrawElements - Submit Draw Call]
    S --> T[GPU Vertex Shader - Per Vertex]
    T --> U[Primitive Assembly - 2 Triangles]
    U --> V[Rasterisation - Fragments]
    V --> W[GPU Fragment Shader - Per Pixel]
    W --> X[Framebuffer - Final Pixels on Screen]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid: Rectangle Triangulation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Rectangle 4 Corners] --> B[Triangle 1 - Top-Right, Bottom-Right, Top-Left]
    A --> C[Triangle 2 - Bottom-Right, Bottom-Left, Top-Left]
    B --> D[glDrawElements - 6 indices]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Screen Rectangle Rendered]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid: VAO State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NoContext: glfwInit
    NoContext --> ActiveContext: glfwMakeContextCurrent
    ActiveContext --> GlewReady: glewInit
    GlewReady --> VAOCreated: glGenVertexArrays
    VAOCreated --> VAOBound: glBindVertexArray
    VAOBound --> VBOBound: glBindBuffer GL_ARRAY_BUFFER
    VBOBound --> DataUploaded: glBufferData
    DataUploaded --> AttrConfigured: glVertexAttribPointer
    AttrConfigured --> AttrEnabled: glEnableVertexAttribArray
    AttrEnabled --> DrawReady: glUseProgram
    DrawReady --> Drawing: glDrawElements
    Drawing --> [*]: glfwTerminate
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Memory Layout of a Vertex

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[VBO Memory Block] --> B[Vertex 0]
    A --> C[Vertex 1]
    A --> D[Vertex 2]
    A --> E[Vertex 3]
    B --> F[Px Py Pz - 12 bytes]
    B --> G[Cr Cg Cb - 12 bytes]
    C --> H[Px Py Pz - 12 bytes]
    C --> I[Cr Cg Cb - 12 bytes]
    D --> J[Px Py Pz - 12 bytes]
    D --> K[Cr Cg Cb - 12 bytes]
    E --> L[Px Py Pz - 12 bytes]
    E --> M[Cr Cg Cb - 12 bytes]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Strided Layout Explained:** Each vertex occupies 24 contiguous bytes. The `glVertexAttribPointer` for *position* uses `offset = 0`, while *colour* uses `offset = 12`. This is the **interleaved attribute layout**, the industry-standard pattern in VBO design.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q1.** What is the role of GLEW in an OpenGL program? Why must `glewInit()` be called *after* a GL context is made current?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
GLEW (OpenGL Extension Wrangler) is a cross-platform C/C++ library that **resolves and loads modern OpenGL extension function pointers** at runtime. Most operating systems (especially Windows) expose only the OpenGL 1.1 API by default, so functions like `glGenBuffers`, `glCreateShader`, and `glDrawElements` are not directly available. GLEW scans the GPU driver for supported extensions and populates the function pointer table, exposing these modern features. (1 mark)

`glewInit()` queries the active OpenGL context to determine which functions are available; therefore a current context **must exist** before the call, otherwise GLEW cannot bind the function pointers to the correct driver. If called before `glfwMakeContextCurrent`, `glewInit()` returns `GLEW_NO_CONTEXT_ERROR`. (2 marks)

---

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q2.** Differentiate between a VBO, VAO, and EBO with one-line definitions.

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark each):**

| Object | One-Line Definition |
|---|---|
| **VBO** | A raw GPU memory buffer that stores vertex data (positions, colours, UVs). |
| **VAO** | A state-object that *stores* the attribute pointer configuration of one or more VBOs. |
| **EBO** | An index buffer that stores vertex indices to enable vertex re-use. |

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module-Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

---

#### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step process of creating and using a **Vertex Buffer Object (VBO)** in modern OpenGL. Include all relevant `glGen*`, `glBind*`, and `glBufferData` calls. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete GLSL **vertex shader** and **fragment shader** pair that renders a rectangle in **orange** colour. State the GLSL version directive and explain each line. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) VBO Creation Steps (7 Marks):**

1. **Generate** a buffer ID using `glGenBuffers(1, &VBO);` — this allocates an unused GPU buffer name. (1 mark)
2. **Bind** the buffer to the `GL_ARRAY_BUFFER` target using `glBindBuffer(GL_ARRAY_BUFFER, VBO);` — subsequent buffer operations apply to this object. (1 mark)
3. **Upload** vertex data using `glBufferData(GL_ARRAY_BUFFER, sizeof(vertices), vertices, GL_STATIC_DRAW);` — copies the host array into GPU memory. (1 mark)
4. `GL_STATIC_DRAW` hints that the data will be uploaded once and drawn many times, allowing the driver to place it in fast VRAM. (1 mark)
5. **Define attribute layout** with `glVertexAttribPointer(0, 3, GL_FLOAT, GL_FALSE, stride, offset);` — tells the GPU *how* to read the buffer. (1 mark)
6. **Enable** the attribute with `glEnableVertexAttribArray(0);` — without this call, the attribute is disabled. (1 mark)
7. **Unbind** with `glBindBuffer(GL_ARRAY_BUFFER, 0);` to prevent accidental modification; a VAO is recommended to persist the configuration across binds. (1 mark)

---

**(b) Shader Pair for Orange Rectangle (7 Marks):**

```glsl
#version 330 core                        // (1) GLSL version + core profile

layout (location = 0) in vec3 aPos;      // (2) Input: vertex position, attribute slot 0

void main()                              // (3) Entry point
{
    gl_Position = vec4(aPos, 1.0);       // (4) Output: clip-space position (homogeneous)
}
```

```glsl
#version 330 core                        // (1) GLSL version + core profile
out vec4 FragColor;                      // (2) Output colour to framebuffer

void main()
{
    FragColor = vec4(1.0, 0.5, 0.2, 1.0); // (3) Orange: R=1.0, G=0.5, B=0.2
}
```

**Line-by-line Explanation (7 marks distributed):**
- Line 1: `#version 330 core` selects **OpenGL 3.3 core profile** — required for `in`/`out` qualifiers and layout locations. (1)
- Line 3: `layout (location = 0)` binds the attribute to **vertex attribute slot 0**, matching the C++ `glEnableVertexAttribArray(0)` call. (1)
- Line 4: `vec3 aPos` declares a 3-component float position input. (1)
- Line 8: `vec4(aPos, 1.0)` creates a homogeneous coordinate with $w = 1$ (no perspective division effect). (1)
- Line 12: `out vec4 FragColor` is the only output in fragment shaders. (1)
- Line 17: `vec4(1.0, 0.5, 0.2, 1.0)` represents the orange colour in normalised RGB. (1)
- Line 18: Alpha is 1.0 (fully opaque). (1)

---

#### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**(a)** With a neat diagram and C++ code snippet, explain how a **rectangle is constructed from two triangles** using an **EBO** in OpenGL. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **glDrawArrays** and **glDrawElements**. Under what circumstances would you prefer one over the other? Provide a real-game scenario for each. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Rectangle via Two Triangles + EBO (7 Marks):**

A rectangle has **4 unique vertices** and **6 indices** (3 per triangle). The two triangles share a diagonal edge.

**Vertex array (4 unique positions, 3 floats each):**
```cpp
GLfloat vertices[] = {
     0.5f,  0.5f, 0.0f,   // index 0 : top-right
     0.5f, -0.5f, 0.0f,   // index 1 : bottom-right
    -0.5f, -0.5f, 0.0f,   // index 2 : bottom-left
    -0.5f,  0.5f, 0.0f    // index 3 : top-left
};
GLuint indices[] = {
    0, 1, 3,   // first triangle
    1, 2, 3    // second triangle
};
```

The EBO is created identically to a VBO except it is bound to `GL_ELEMENT_ARRAY_BUFFER` and contains integer indices. `glDrawElements(GL_TRIANGLES, 6, GL_UNSIGNED_INT, 0);` then issues the draw call. (3 marks for code)

**Diagram description (2 marks):**

```
        (0,0.5)●─────●(0.5,0.5)
              │\     │
              │ \ T1 │
              │  \   │
              │   \  │
              │ T2 \ │
              │     \│
       (-0.5,-0.5)───●(0.5,-0.5)

T1 = {0, 1, 3}   T2 = {1, 2, 3}
```

**EBO advantages (2 marks):**
- Saves **vertex shader invocations** (6 → 4, a 33% reduction).
- Reduces GPU memory bandwidth for attributes like normals, UVs, tangents.
- Critical for meshes (cube, sphere, terrain) where many triangles share vertices.

---

**(b) Comparison: glDrawArrays vs glDrawElements (7 Marks):**

| Aspect | `glDrawArrays` | `glDrawElements` |
|---|---|---|
| Data source | Sequential vertices in VBO | Index list in EBO |
| Vertex re-use | None — each vertex uploaded as many times as drawn | Yes — vertex uploaded once, referenced by index |
| Memory | Higher (duplicates per triangle) | Lower (unique vertices only) |
| Driver complexity | Lower | Higher (driver fetches via index) |
| Best for | Particle systems, dynamic point clouds, full-screen quads (with `GL_TRIANGLE_STRIP` tricks) | Static meshes: terrain, characters, buildings |

**Real-game scenarios (2 marks):**
- `glDrawArrays`: rendering **particles** in a fire spell (each particle is a 6-vertex quad; no sharing).
- `glDrawElements`: rendering a **terrain mesh** with thousands of shared vertices across a heightmap.

**Decision rule (1 mark):** Use `glDrawElements` whenever the geometry is **static and has shared vertices**; use `glDrawArrays` for **dynamic per-frame geometry** where EBO updates would cost more than the savings.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deductions**
>
> 1. **Forgetting `glewExperimental = GL_TRUE`** → `glewInit()` returns `GLEW_OK` but `glGenBuffers` is still `NULL` → **2-mark deduction** for "incomplete setup".
> 2. **Mismatched `location` indices** between `glVertexAttribPointer(0, ...)` and `layout (location = 0) in vec3 aPos;` → GPU draws nothing or reads garbage → **3-mark deduction** for "incorrect attribute binding".
> 3. **Calling `glewInit()` before `glfwMakeContextCurrent()`** → silent failures, undefined behaviour → **1-mark deduction**.
> 4. **Confusing `GL_ARRAY_BUFFER` with `GL_ELEMENT_ARRAY_BUFFER`** when binding an EBO → crashes or wrong geometry → **2-mark deduction**.
> 5. **Drawing 6 vertices instead of 6 indices** with `glDrawArrays` for a rectangle — works, but wastes 2 vertex-shader invocations; examiners expect the **EBO approach** explicitly mentioned in Module 4 → **2-mark deduction** for using non-indexed.
> 6. **Forgetting `#version 330 core`** in GLSL → driver falls back to legacy syntax, compilation errors on `in`/`out` → **2-mark deduction**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✍️

- 📌 **GLEW** is a *runtime* extension loader, not an OpenGL replacement.
- 📌 `glewInit()` **must be called after** the GL context is current.
- 📌 Set `glewExperimental = GL_TRUE` to expose **core-profile** functions reliably.
- 📌 The **VBO** holds raw data; the **VAO** holds the *interpretation* of that data.
- 📌 A **rectangle** = **2 triangles** = **6 indices** (or 6 vertices without EBO).
- 📌 EBO reduces **vertex shader invocations**, the most expensive per-vertex stage.
- 📌 **Stride** in `glVertexAttribPointer` = bytes per vertex (sum of all attribute sizes).
- 📌 **Offset** for the $k$-th attribute = (sum of sizes of preceding attributes) × `sizeof(type)`.
- 📌 `GL_STATIC_DRAW` is the correct usage hint for **uploaded once, drawn many**.
- 📌 A complete shader pipeline needs **at minimum** a vertex + fragment shader.
- 📌 `glUseProgram(prog)` activates the program; `glBindVertexArray(VAO)` activates geometry.
- 📌 `glDrawElements` requires a **bound EBO** *inside* an **active VAO** — losing the VAO loses the EBO binding.
- 📌 In KTU 2024 exams, always mention **GLSL version directive** (`#version 330 core`) when writing shaders.
- 📌 Always **check compile and link status** with `glGetShaderiv` / `glGetProgramiv` in production code.
